parts.content: Describe how activation energy relates to the energy profile diagram.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** that activation energy is the minimum energy required for reactants to reach the transition state <marks>A1</marks>
- **Identify** that on the diagram, it is the energy difference between the reactants and the peak (transition state) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define activation energy conceptually
steps.content: In chemistry, for a reaction to occur, reactant particles must collide with sufficient energy to break existing bonds. This **activation energy ($E_a$)** is defined as the minimum kinetic energy required for reactants to undergo a successful collision and reach the **transition state**.

steps.title: Identify $E_a$ on the graph
steps.content:
Visually, the activation energy is the "energy barrier" or the "hill" that the reactants must climb. On an energy profile diagram, it is the **vertical distance** measured from the energy level of the **reactants** to the **peak** of the curve (which represents the transition state).

$$\text{Activation Energy } (E_a) = \text{Energy of Transition State} - \text{Energy of Reactants}$$

steps.title: Relate to the photosynthesis diagram
steps.content: In the provided diagram for photosynthesis, you can see that the reactants ($6\mathrm{CO}_2$ and $6\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$) start at a lower energy level. To reach the products, they must first gain enough energy (from light) to reach the highest point on the graph. The difference between that starting line and the peak is the activation energy.

parts.content: Explain why photosynthesis is an endothermic process, referring to both enthalpy change and the energy diagram.
parts.markscheme:
- **Explain** that photosynthesis is endothermic because the products have a higher potential energy than the reactants <marks>A1</marks>
- **State** that the enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) is positive, indicating energy absorption <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze energy levels
steps.content:
To understand why photosynthesis is endothermic, we first look at the vertical axis of the energy profile diagram, which represents **potential energy**. 

In the provided diagram, notice that the energy level of the **products** (glucose and oxygen) is significantly higher than the energy level of the **reactants** (carbon dioxide and water). This indicates that the products have a higher chemical potential energy than the starting materials.

highlights.text: products have a higher potential energy than the reactants

steps.title: Determine enthalpy change sign
steps.content:
The enthalpy change, denoted as $\Delta H$, represents the difference in energy between the products and the reactants. It is calculated as:

$$\Delta H = H_{\text{products}} - H_{\text{reactants}}$$

Because the energy of the products is greater than the energy of the reactants in this reaction, the value of $\Delta H$ must be **positive** ($ \Delta H > 0 $). This is a defining characteristic of an endothermic process.

steps.title: Explain energy absorption
steps.content:
For the reactants to reach the higher energy state of the products, the system must **absorb energy** from its surroundings. 

In photosynthesis, this energy is provided by sunlight (as indicated by "light energy" over the reaction arrow). Since energy is taken in from the environment to drive the reaction forward, the process is classified as **endothermic**.

highlights.text: enthalpy change (\Delta H) is positive, indicating energy absorption

parts.content: Explain the role of light energy in this reaction with reference to the activation energy.
parts.markscheme:
- **Explain** that light provides the energy required to overcome the activation energy barrier ($E_a$) <marks>A1</marks>
- **State** that this allows the reactants to reach the transition state and for the reaction to proceed <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Recall Collision Theory requirements
steps.content:
To understand this reaction, we first look at **Topic R2.2: Collision theory**. For a chemical reaction to occur, reactant particles must collide with a minimum amount of energy. This threshold is known as the **activation energy** ($E_a$). 

Without reaching this energy level, the reactant molecules ($\mathrm{CO}_2$ and $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$) will simply bounce off each other without reacting.

steps.title: Light as an energy source
steps.content:
The energy profile diagram shows a significant barrier between the reactants and products. The **light energy** absorbed during photosynthesis provides the external energy needed for the reactants to overcome this **high activation energy ($E_a$)** barrier.

Basically, the light provides the "boost" needed to get the molecules moving or vibrating with enough energy to break their existing bonds.

steps.title: Reaching the transition state
steps.content: By providing the necessary energy to meet the $E_a$, light allows the reactants to reach the **transition state** (the peak of the curve). At this state, the old bonds are breaking and new bonds are beginning to form, allowing the reaction to proceed and ultimately produce glucose and oxygen.

steps.title: Analyze the energy profile
steps.content:
In this energy profile diagram, notice that the reactants (at the start) are at a lower energy level than the products. To transform into products, the reactants must first climb a large "hill" on the graph. This hill represents the **activation energy** ($E_a$), which is the minimum energy required for a chemical reaction to occur.

This concept is a key part of **Topic R2.2: Activation energy** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Overcome the energy barrier
steps.content: The first mark is awarded for explaining that light energy provides the specific energy input needed to overcome this barrier. In the diagram, the arrow labeled "light energy" indicates that the absorption of light gives the reactants enough energy to reach the top of the activation energy ($E_a$) curve.

steps.title: Reach the transition state
steps.content: The second mark is for stating the result of this energy input. By providing enough energy to overcome $E_a$, the light energy allows the reactants to reach the **transition state** at the peak of the curve. Once the reactants reach this state, bonds can break and reform, allowing the reaction to proceed and form the products.

parts.content: Describe how the energy profile diagram would change if an enzyme (biological catalyst) were used.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** that an enzyme would lower the activation energy, making the curve rise to a lower peak <marks>A1</marks>
- **State** that the overall energy change ($\Delta H$) remains the same <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define the enzyme's role
steps.content: In chemistry, an enzyme acts as a biological catalyst. According to **Topic R2.2.5 (Catalysts)** in your syllabus, a catalyst works by providing an alternative reaction pathway that requires less energy to get started.

steps.title: Lowering the activation energy
steps.content: Because the enzyme provides a pathway with a lower **activation energy** ($E_a$), the curve on the energy profile diagram would not rise as high. This results in a **lower peak** compared to the original uncatalyzed reaction.

steps.title: Analyze the enthalpy change
steps.content: It is crucial to remember that a catalyst does not change the energy of the reactants or the energy of the products. Since the starting and ending energy levels remain the same, the **enthalpy change** ($\Delta H$) of the reaction is **unaffected**.

steps.title: Summarize for marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, you must mention both qualitative changes:
1. The peak of the curve is **lowered** (due to lower $E_a$).
2. The vertical distance between reactants and products ($\Delta H$) **remains the same**.

highlights.text: enthalpy change (\Delta H) remains the same

parts.content:
The Arrhenius equation is given by:

$$k = A e^{-\frac{E_a}{RT}}$$

Using this equation, explain how a decrease in $E_a$ by the enzyme affects the rate constant $k$ at constant temperature.
parts.markscheme:
- **Explain** that a decrease in $E_a$ makes the exponent $\left( -\frac{E_a}{RT} \right)$ less negative (or larger) <marks>M1</marks>
- **Conclude** that therefore, $k$ increases, meaning the reaction rate increases <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the Arrhenius equation
steps.content:
To explain the effect of an enzyme on the rate constant $k$, we first look at the Arrhenius equation provided in the data booklet:

$$k=A e^{\frac{-E_{a}}{R T}}$$

Where:
- $k$ is the rate constant.
- $A$ is the pre-exponential factor.
- $E_a$ is the activation energy.
- $R$ is the gas constant.
- $T$ is the absolute temperature.

This formula is covered in **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**.

steps.title: Analyze the exponent
steps.content:
An enzyme works by providing an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy ($E_a$). 

If we keep the temperature ($T$) constant, a **decrease** in $E_a$ means that the fraction $\frac{E_a}{RT}$ becomes a smaller positive number. 

Consequently, the exponent term, which is the negative of this fraction $\left( -\frac{E_a}{RT} \right)$, becomes **less negative** (or mathematically larger).

highlights.text: decrease in $E_a$ makes the exponent $\left( -\frac{E_a}{RT} \right)$ less negative (or larger)

steps.title: Determine the effect on k
steps.content:
In the function $e^x$, as $x$ increases, the value of $e^x$ also increases. 

Since the exponent $\left( -\frac{E_a}{RT} \right)$ has become less negative (larger), the entire exponential factor $e^{-\frac{E_a}{RT}}$ increases. 

Because $k$ is directly proportional to this factor ($k = A \times e^{-\frac{E_a}{RT}}$), the value of the **rate constant $k$ increases**, which ultimately increases the overall reaction rate.

highlights.text: k increases, meaning the reaction rate increases

steps.title: Start with Arrhenius equation
steps.content:
To understand the relationship between the activation energy ($E_a$) and the rate constant ($k$), we begin with the exponential form of the Arrhenius equation provided in the question and your data booklet:

$$k = A e^{-\frac{E_a}{RT}}$$

This equation is a key part of **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**.

steps.title: Apply logarithmic transformation
steps.content:
We can transform this into a linear form by taking the natural logarithm ($\ln$) of both sides of the equation. This makes it much easier to see the mathematical relationship:

$$\ln k = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{RT}$$

In this form, $\ln k$ is expressed as a constant ($\ln A$) minus a specific term ($\frac{E_a}{RT}$).

steps.title: Analyze the energy term
steps.content:
Now, let's look at what happens when an enzyme is added. An enzyme provides an alternative pathway with a lower activation energy ($E_a$). 

At a constant temperature ($T$), if $E_a$ decreases, the value of the entire term $\frac{E_a}{RT}$ also decreases. Since we are *subtracting* this term from $\ln A$, subtracting a smaller number results in a larger value for $\ln k$. 

Mathematically, the exponent term $-\frac{E_a}{RT}$ becomes **less negative** (or larger).

steps.title: Conclude the effect
steps.content:
Because $\ln k$ has increased, the rate constant $k$ itself must also increase. 

A larger rate constant $k$ directly corresponds to a faster reaction rate, which is why enzymes are so effective at speeding up biological processes like photosynthesis.

questions.specification:
The decomposition of phosphorus pentachloride is represented by the following equilibrium equation:

$$\text{PCl}_5(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{PCl}_3(g) + \text{Cl}_2(g)$$

At a particular temperature, the equilibrium constant, $K_c$, for this reaction is $0.040 \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$. In an equilibrium mixture at this temperature, the concentration of $\text{PCl}_5$ is $0.10 \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$ and the concentration of $\text{PCl}_3$ is $0.020 \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$.

What is the concentration of $\text{Cl}_2$ at equilibrium?

questions.specification:
The catalytic decomposition of hydrogen peroxide solution is studied by monitoring the decrease in mass as oxygen gas is released. The reaction is catalyzed by manganese(IV) oxide powder. The chemical equation for the process is:

$$2\text{H}_2\text{O}_2(\text{aq}) \xrightarrow{\text{MnO}_2(\text{s})} 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(\text{l}) + \text{O}_2(\text{g})$$

A student recorded the following data during the experiment:

| Time (s) | Mass of flask and contents (g) |
| :--- | :--- |
| 0 | 120.00 |
| 30 | 119.55 |
| 60 | 119.25 |
| 90 | 119.10 |
| 120 | 119.05 |
| 150 | 119.05 |

labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.5 Catalysts
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (c) and (d) focus on the effect of MnO2 as a catalyst on rate and why the final mass is unchanged.

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (d) explicitly asks for an explanation using collision theory.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.4 Activation energy
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) asks for the definition of activation energy; also relevant to explaining catalytic rate increases.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.3 Factors affecting the rate of reaction
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (e) changes hydrogen peroxide concentration and asks how results change, testing factors affecting rate (concentration, surface area via catalyst amount).

labeledSubSubTopics.title: S1.4.5 Molar concentration
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (e) involves changing solution concentration (0.5 to 1.0 mol dm−3) and linking this to observed rate/curve shape.

parts.content: State what is meant by the term 'activation energy' ($$E_a$$).
parts.markscheme: The minimum energy that colliding particles must possess for a reaction to occur. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using the data provided in the table, calculate the average rate of reaction in terms of mass loss during the first $30$ seconds of the experiment.
parts.markscheme: Rate $= \frac{120.00\text{ g} - 119.55\text{ g}}{30\text{ s}} = 0.015\text{ g s}^{-1}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Suggest why adding a larger mass of manganese(IV) oxide catalyst would increase the rate of reaction but not change the final mass of the flask.
parts.markscheme: Catalysts are not consumed in the reaction, so they do not change the amount of product formed/yield. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain, in terms of the collision theory, how the addition of manganese(IV) oxide increases the rate of decomposition of hydrogen peroxide.
parts.markscheme:
- The catalyst provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Therefore, a greater proportion/fraction of collisions have energy greater than or equal to the activation energy (resulting in more successful collisions per unit time). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: The original experiment used $50\text{ cm}^3$ of $0.5\text{ mol dm}^{-3}$ hydrogen peroxide. Sketch a graph on the provided axes to show the results if the experiment were repeated using $50\text{ cm}^3$ of $1.0\text{ mol dm}^{-3}$ hydrogen peroxide. Label the axes and your curve.
parts.markscheme:
- Curve starts at the same initial mass ($120.00\text{ g}$). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Curve has a steeper initial gradient than the original data (indicating a faster rate). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Curve levels off at a lower final mass (approximately $118.10\text{ g}$) because more oxygen is produced. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe and explain the change in the rate of reaction as the time increases from $0$ to $120$ seconds.
parts.markscheme:
- The rate decreases over time (the gradient of the mass-loss curve becomes less steep). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This is because the concentration of hydrogen peroxide decreases as it is used up, leading to a lower frequency of collisions. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain why the student placed a plug of cotton wool in the neck of the flask during the experiment.
parts.markscheme:
- To prevent the loss of liquid spray/droplets from the flask (which would lead to an overestimation of gas mass). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- While allowing the oxygen gas to escape freely. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
The industrial synthesis of methanol is a reversible process involving the hydrogenation of carbon monoxide. The reaction is represented by the following equilibrium equation:

$$\mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g}) + 2\mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{OH}(\mathrm{g}) \quad \Delta H = -91 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.3 How far? The extent of chemical change > R2.3.2 The equilibrium law and constant
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.3.2 The equilibrium law and constant
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (a), (b) and (d) focus on writing the Kc expression and calculating Kc from equilibrium concentrations, which is the equilibrium law and constant.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.3 How far? The extent of chemical change > R2.3.3 Magnitude of K and temperature dependence
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.3.3 Magnitude of K and temperature dependence
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (a) and (c) address what a large Kc implies and how Kc changes with temperature for an exothermic reaction (temperature dependence and magnitude of K).

parts.content: Outline what a very large value of the equilibrium constant ($$K_c \gg 1$$) indicates about the extent of a chemical reaction.
parts.markscheme: The reaction goes «almost» to completion / the equilibrium position lies far to the right / products are strongly favored over reactants. <marks> 1 mark </marks>

parts.content: Formulate the equilibrium constant expression ($$K_c$$) for the synthesis of methanol.
parts.markscheme: $$K_c = \frac{[\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{OH}]}{[\mathrm{CO}][\mathrm{H}_2]^2}$$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

parts.content: Predict, with a reason, how an increase in temperature will affect the value of the equilibrium constant, $$K_c$$.
parts.markscheme:
Value of $$K_c$$ decreases <marks> 1 mark </marks>

Forward reaction is exothermic / equilibrium shifts to the left «to oppose the increase in temperature» <marks> 1 mark </marks>

parts.content:
At a specific temperature, the equilibrium concentrations of the species involved in the reaction are shown in the table below.

| Species | Equilibrium concentration / $$\mathrm{mol\,dm}^{-3}$$ |
| :--- | :--- |
| $$\mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})$$ | $$0.20$$ |
| $$\mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{g})$$ | $$0.40$$ |
| $$\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{OH}(\mathrm{g})$$ | $$0.80$$ |

Calculate the value of $$K_c$$ at this temperature.
parts.markscheme:
$$K_c = \frac{0.80}{(0.20) \times (0.40)^2}$$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

$$K_c = 25$$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>
<note>Accept $$25.0$$. Units are not required.</note>

questions.specification:
The oxidation of sulfur dioxide to sulfur trioxide is an essential industrial step in the manufacture of sulfuric acid via the Contact process.

$$2\text{SO}_2(\text{g}) + \text{O}_2(\text{g}) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{SO}_3(\text{g})$$

Relevant thermodynamic data for the species involved are provided in the table below:

| Species | $\Delta H_f^\ominus\ (\text{kJ mol}^{-1})$ | $S^\ominus\ (\text{J K}^{-1} \text{mol}^{-1})$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| $\text{SO}_2(\text{g})$ | $-296.8$ | $248.2$ |
| $\text{O}_2(\text{g})$ | $0$ | $205.2$ |
| $\text{SO}_3(\text{g})$ | $-395.7$ | $256.8$ |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 1. What drives chemical reactions? > R1.4 Entropy and spontaneity (HL) > R1.4.3 Spontaneity and Gibbs free energy (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R1.4.3 Spontaneity and Gibbs free energy (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (d) and (e) use ΔG° = ΔH° − TΔS° to find the temperature for non-spontaneity and to calculate ΔG° at 1100 K; also involves interpreting spontaneity from ΔG°.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 1. What drives chemical reactions? > R1.4 Entropy and spontaneity (HL) > R1.4.1 Entropy (S) (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R1.4.1 Entropy (S) (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (a) and (c) require predicting and calculating the standard entropy change ΔS° from standard molar entropies, including sign reasoning from gas moles.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 1. What drives chemical reactions? > R1.1 Measuring enthalpy change > R1.1.4 Standard enthalpy change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R1.1.4 Standard enthalpy change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) calculates the standard enthalpy change ΔH° for the reaction using standard enthalpies of formation, i.e., standard reaction enthalpy.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 1. What drives chemical reactions? > R1.4 Entropy and spontaneity (HL) > R1.4.4 Gibbs free energy and equilibrium (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R1.4.4 Gibbs free energy and equilibrium (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (f) connects ΔG° at a given temperature to whether K is greater or less than 1 (ΔG° = −RT ln K), relating Gibbs free energy to equilibrium.

parts.content: Predict, with a reason, the sign of the standard entropy change ($\Delta S^\ominus$) for this reaction.
parts.markscheme: Negative AND there is a decrease in the number of moles of gas (from 3 moles of reactants to 2 moles of products) / decrease in disorder. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the standard enthalpy change ($\Delta H^\ominus$) for the reaction in $\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$.
parts.markscheme:
$\Delta H^\ominus = \sum \Delta H_f^\ominus(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H_f^\ominus(\text{reactants})$
$\Delta H^\ominus = 2(-395.7) - [2(-296.8) + 0] = -197.8\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the standard entropy change ($\Delta S^\ominus$) for the reaction in $\text{J K}^{-1} \text{mol}^{-1}$.
parts.markscheme:
$\Delta S^\ominus = \sum S^\ominus(\text{products}) - \sum S^\ominus(\text{reactants})$

$\Delta S^\ominus = 2(256.8) - [2(248.2) + 205.2] = -188.0\ \text{J K}^{-1} \text{mol}^{-1}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
Determine the temperature, in K, at which the reaction becomes non-spontaneous. Assume that $\Delta H^\ominus$ and $\Delta S^\ominus$ are independent of temperature.

If you did not obtain answers in Part 2 and Part 3, use $-190.0\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ and $-180.0\ \text{J K}^{-1} \text{mol}^{-1}$ respectively, but these are not the correct answers.
parts.markscheme:
Reaction becomes non-spontaneous when $\Delta G^\ominus = 0$, so $T = \frac{\Delta H^\ominus}{\Delta S^\ominus}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

$T = \frac{-197800\ \text{J mol}^{-1}}{-188.0\ \text{J K}^{-1} \text{mol}^{-1}} = 1052\ \text{K}$

Accept $1050\ \text{K}$ to $1053\ \text{K}$ depending on rounding. <marks>1 mark</marks>

If ECF values used: $T = \frac{-190000}{-180.0} = 1056\ \text{K}$

parts.content: Calculate the Gibbs free energy change ($\Delta G^\ominus$), in $\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$, for this reaction at $1100\ \text{K}$.
parts.markscheme:
$\Delta G^\ominus = \Delta H^\ominus - T\Delta S^\ominus$

$\Delta G^\ominus = -197.8\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1} - (1100\ \text{K} \times -0.1880\ \text{kJ K}^{-1} \text{mol}^{-1})$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

$\Delta G^\ominus = +9.0\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

If ECF values from Part 4 placeholder used: $\Delta G^\ominus = -190.0 - (1100 \times -0.1800) = +8.0\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$

parts.content: Predict, with a reason, whether the value of the equilibrium constant ($K$) will be greater than or less than 1 at $1100\ \text{K}$.
parts.markscheme: $K < 1$ AND $\Delta G^\ominus$ is positive / reaction is non-spontaneous (so $\ln K$ must be negative). <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:

$$\mathrm{CH}_4(\text{g}) + \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}(\text{g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CO}(\text{g}) + 3\mathrm{H}_2(\text{g}) \qquad \Delta H = +206 \, \mathrm{kJ\,mol^{-1}}$$

Which row in the table correctly shows the effect of increasing the temperature of the system on the equilibrium yield of hydrogen and the value of the equilibrium constant, $K_c$?

| | Equilibrium yield of hydrogen | Value of $K_c$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **A** | Increases | Increases |
| **B** | Decreases | Decreases |
| **C** | Increases | Stays constant |
| **D** | Decreases | Stays constant |

An unknown organic compound, $X$, has the molecular formula $\mathrm{C_3H_6O}$. The following Infrared (IR) spectroscopic data was obtained for a sample of the compound:

| Absorption Band ($\text{cm}^{-1}$) | Intensity |
| :--- | :--- |
| $2850-2980$ | Strong |
| $1710-1750$ | Strong |
| $1050-1300$ | Medium |

Note: No significant absorption was observed in the $3200-3600\text{ cm}^{-1}$ region.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: S3.2.8 Mass spectrometry (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (b) and (c) use mass spectrometry: using fragmentation patterns to distinguish propanal vs propanone and identifying the molecular ion (M+) m/z from the molecular formula.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Structure 3. Classification of matter > S3.2 Functional groups: Classification of organic compounds > S3.2.2 Functional groups
labeledSubSubTopics.title: S3.2.2 Functional groups
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (a) and (d) involve identifying and classifying functional groups (aldehyde/ketone; propanone as a ketone) from formula/spectral evidence and structure.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Structure 2. Models of bonding and structure > S2.2 The covalent model > S2.2.9 Intermolecular forces and physical properties
labeledSubSubTopics.title: S2.2.9 Intermolecular forces and physical properties
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (e) compares boiling point and water solubility via intermolecular forces (dipole–dipole, hydrogen bonding with water, London forces) and links these forces to physical properties.

parts.content: Identify the functional group present in compound $X$. Justify your answer using the provided IR data, specifically explaining why the sample cannot be an alcohol.
parts.markscheme:
- The functional group is a carbonyl group ($\mathrm{C=O}$) / compound $X$ is a ketone or aldehyde <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The strong peak at $1710-1750\text{ cm}^{-1}$ indicates $\mathrm{C=O}$, and the absence of a broad peak at $3200-3600\text{ cm}^{-1}$ rules out the hydroxyl ($\mathrm{O-H}$) group found in alcohols <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Compound $X$ could be either propanal or propanone. Explain how the fragmentation pattern in a mass spectrum can be used to distinguish between these two functional group isomers.
parts.markscheme:
- Propanone would produce a significant fragment at $m/z = 43$ (corresponding to $\mathrm{[CH_3CO]^+}$) or $m/z = 15$ ($\mathrm{[CH_3]}^+$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Propanal would produce a significant fragment at $m/z = 29$ (corresponding to $\mathrm{[CHO]^+}$ or $\mathrm{[C_2H_5]}^+$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State the $m/z$ ratio of the molecular ion ($M^+$) peak observed in the mass spectrum of compound $X$.
parts.markscheme: - $m/z = 58$ (accept $58.09$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Draw the full structural formula for propanone and identify the specific class of organic compounds it belongs to.
parts.markscheme:
- Correct drawing of propanone showing all atoms and bonds ($\mathrm{CH_3COCH_3}$ structure) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identifies the class as ketones <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Compare the boiling point and solubility in water of propanone with those of butane ($\mathrm{C_4H_{10}}$), which has a similar molar mass. Justify your answer by referencing the types of intermolecular forces present in each compound.
parts.markscheme:
- Propanone has a higher boiling point than butane <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Propanone is polar and experiences (permanent) dipole-dipole attractions, whereas butane is non-polar and only experiences weak London dispersion forces <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Propanone is more soluble in water than butane because the lone pairs on its carbonyl oxygen can form hydrogen bonds with water molecules <marks>1 mark</marks>

| Wavenumber ($\text{cm}^{-1}$) | Intensity | Shape |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $2980\text{--}2850$ | Strong | Sharp |
| $2250$ | Medium | Sharp |

| $1000\text{--}600$ | Variable | Multiple peaks |

labeledSubSubTopics.title: S3.2.9 Infrared spectra (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: All parts focus on interpreting IR absorption peaks (e.g., 2250 cm⁻¹ nitrile), comparing spectra including fingerprint region, and predicting spectral changes during hydrolysis and between different functional groups.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: S3.2.2 Functional groups
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Identifying and contrasting functional groups (nitrile vs carboxylic acid vs amine) is required to explain which IR features appear/disappear and why.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Structure 2. Models of bonding and structure > S2.2 The covalent model > S2.2.2 Single, double and triple bonds
labeledSubSubTopics.title: S2.2.2 Single, double and triple bonds
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) asks for an explanation of why a C≡N triple bond absorbs at higher wavenumber than a C–N single bond, linking to bond order/strength.

parts.content: Identify the functional group in Compound **X** that is responsible for the absorption observed at $2250\text{ cm}^{-1}$.
parts.markscheme: Identify the presence of a nitrile group (or $\text{C}\equiv\text{N}$ triple bond). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain why the $\text{C}\equiv\text{N}$ triple bond absorbs at a significantly higher wavenumber ($2250\text{ cm}^{-1}$) than a $\text{C-N}$ single bond ($1000\text{--}1200\text{ cm}^{-1}$).
parts.markscheme:
State that the triple bond is stronger than the single bond (higher force constant). <marks>1 mark</marks>

Explain that higher bond strength increases the frequency/wavenumber of the vibration. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Assess the importance of the region below $1500\text{ cm}^{-1}$ when comparing the spectrum of Compound **X** to a known sample of propanenitrile.
parts.markscheme:
Identify this as the "fingerprint region," which contains a unique pattern of bending and stretching vibrations for a specific molecule. <marks>1 mark</marks>

State that an exact match in this region between the unknown and the reference confirms the identity of the compound beyond just identifying functional groups. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
Compound **X** can undergo acid hydrolysis to form propanoic acid ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_6\text{O}_2$). 

Outline the changes you would expect to see in the IR spectrum as the reaction proceeds to completion.
parts.markscheme:
Describe the disappearance of the sharp nitrile peak at $\approx 2250\text{ cm}^{-1}$. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Describe the appearance of a strong, sharp $\text{C=O}$ (carbonyl) peak at $\approx 1700\text{--}1750\text{ cm}^{-1}$. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Describe the appearance of a very broad $\text{O-H}$ (hydroxyl) absorption characteristic of a carboxylic acid in the $2500\text{--}3300\text{ cm}^{-1}$ region. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe how the IR spectrum of propan-1-amine would differ from the spectrum of Compound **X**, and explain the structural reasons for these differences.
parts.markscheme:
Identify that propan-1-amine would lack the $\text{C}\equiv\text{N}$ peak at $2250\text{ cm}^{-1}$ because it contains only single $\text{C-N}$ bonds. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Identify that propan-1-amine would show a new absorption (often a doublet) in the $3300\text{--}3500\text{ cm}^{-1}$ region. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Explain that these peaks arise from $\text{N-H}$ bond stretching vibrations in the amine group, which are absent in the nitrile. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
For the following reaction:

$$ \mathrm{H_2(g)} + \mathrm{CO_2(g)} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H_2O(g)} + \mathrm{CO(g)} \qquad \Delta H = +41 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1} $$

Which change will increase the equilibrium concentration of $$ \mathrm{CO(g)} $$ without changing the value of the equilibrium constant, $$ K_c $$?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.3 How far? The extent of chemical change > R2.3.4 Le Châtelier’s principle
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.3.4 Le Châtelier’s principle
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which change increases equilibrium yield of CO without changing Kc, requiring application of Le Châtelier’s principle to concentration/pressure/catalyst changes.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.3 How far? The extent of chemical change > R2.3.3 Magnitude of K and temperature dependence
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.3.3 Magnitude of K and temperature dependence
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Temperature change affects equilibrium position and the value of Kc for an endothermic reaction (ΔH>0), so distinguishing temperature effects vs other changes is central to the options.

options.markscheme: Adding a reactant shifts the equilibrium to the right, increasing $$\mathrm{CO(g)}$$, while temperature is unchanged so $$K_c$$ is unchanged.

options.content: Increasing the temperature of the system
options.markscheme: Although higher temperature favours the endothermic forward reaction, $$K_c$$ changes with temperature, so this does not satisfy the condition.

options.content: Decreasing the total pressure of the system
options.markscheme: There is no change in total moles of gas ($\Delta n=0$), so changing pressure does not change the equilibrium composition (and thus does not increase $$\mathrm{CO(g)}$$).

options.content: Adding a suitable catalyst to the mixture
options.markscheme: A catalyst speeds up attainment of equilibrium but does not change the equilibrium position or equilibrium amounts, so $$\mathrm{CO(g)}$$ at equilibrium is unchanged.

questions.specification:
The equilibrium constant, $K_{\text{c}}$, for the gaseous reaction $4\text{L} + 2\text{M} \rightleftharpoons 4\text{N} + 2\text{P}$ is $0.0625$ at a specific temperature.

What is the value of $K_{\text{c}}$ for the reaction below at the same temperature?

$$2\text{N} + \text{P} \rightleftharpoons 2\text{L} + \text{M}$$

parts.content: Predict whether the products or reactants are favoured at equilibrium.
parts.markscheme: Products are favoured because $K_c > 1$. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the equilibrium constant
steps.content:
The question provides us with the equilibrium constant, $K_c$, at a specific temperature ($298\text{ K}$).

$$K_c = 2.50 \times 10^4$$

highlights.text: value of K_c for a reaction is 2.50 !!\times 10^4

steps.title: Compare Kc to 1
steps.content:
To determine which side of the reaction is favored, we compare the magnitude of $K_c$ to the threshold value of $1$.

In scientific notation, $2.50 \times 10^4$ is equal to $25,000$. Since $25,000$ is much larger than $1$, we can write:

$$K_c > 1$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the value using scientific notation format.

ti84.instructions: Enter the value using the EE button (2nd + ,).

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to see the numerical result.

calculator.description: Convert scientific notation to standard form to easily compare with 1.

steps.title: Determine the favored side
steps.content:
The equilibrium constant is defined as the ratio of the concentrations of products to reactants. When $K_c > 1$, the numerator (products) must be greater than the denominator (reactants) at equilibrium.

Therefore, the **products** are favoured.

steps.title: Define the concentration ratio
steps.content:
In chemistry, the equilibrium constant $K_c$ is defined by the equilibrium law as the ratio of the concentrations of products to the concentrations of reactants. This is covered in **Topic R2.3: How far? The extent of chemical change**.

A simplified way to look at this ratio is:

$$K_c = \frac{[\text{Products}]}{[\text{Reactants}]}$$

If $K_c$ is much larger than 1, it means the numerator (products) must be significantly larger than the denominator (reactants).

steps.title: Analyze the given value
steps.content:
The question provides a specific value for the equilibrium constant:

$$K_c = 2.50 \times 10^4$$

To see how large this is, we can convert it from scientific notation:

$$2.50 \times 10^4 = 25,000$$

Since $25,000$ is much greater than $1$, the concentration of the substances on the right side of the chemical equation (the products) is much higher than those on the left at equilibrium.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to see the result.

steps.title: Predict the favoured side
steps.content:
Because $K_c > 1$, the equilibrium position lies far to the right. This means the system contains mostly products when it reaches equilibrium.

Therefore, the **products** are favoured.

steps.title: Identify the equilibrium constant
steps.content:
The question provides the equilibrium constant $K_c$ for a reaction at a specific temperature. We can see that the value is significantly greater than $1$.

$$K_c = 2.50 \times 10^4$$

highlights.text: value of $K_c$ for a reaction is $2.50 \times 10^4$

steps.title: Connect K to Gibbs Energy
steps.content:
In IB Chemistry (Topic R1.4/R2.3), we learn the relationship between the standard Gibbs free energy change ($\Delta G^{\ominus}$) and the equilibrium constant ($K$):

$$\Delta G^{\ominus} = -RT \ln K$$

Where:
- $R$ is the gas constant ($8.31\ \text{J K}^{-1} \text{mol}^{-1}$)
- $T$ is the absolute temperature ($298\ \text{K}$)
- $K$ is the equilibrium constant

steps.title: Evaluate the sign of ΔG°
steps.content:
When $K_c > 1$, the natural logarithm ($\ln$) of $K_c$ is a positive number. Because there is a negative sign in the equation $\Delta G^{\ominus} = -RT \ln K$, a positive value for $\ln K$ results in a **negative** value for $\Delta G^{\ominus}$.

$$\ln(2.50 \times 10^4) \approx 10.13$$

gdc.instructions: Enter ln(25000) and execute.

ti84.instructions: Press the LN key, enter 25000, and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type ln(25000) in the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the natural log of the equilibrium constant to determine its sign.

steps.title: Predict favored side
steps.content:
A negative $\Delta G^{\ominus}$ indicates that the reaction is spontaneous in the forward direction under standard conditions. 

Since $K_c$ is much larger than $1$ (leading to a negative $\Delta G^{\ominus}$), the equilibrium position lies far to the right, meaning the **products are favoured**.

parts.content: Calculate $\Delta G^\ominus$ for the reaction, using $R = 8.31\ \text{J mol}^{-1}\ \text{K}^{-1}$.
parts.markscheme:
Substitute the values into the equation $\Delta G^\ominus = -RT \ln K_c$:
$$\Delta G^\ominus = -8.31 \times 298 \times \ln(2.50 \times 10^4)$$ <marks>M1</marks>

$\Delta G^\ominus = -25100\ \text{J mol}^{-1}$ (or $-25.1\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$) <marks>A1</marks>

<note>Award M1 for correct substitution. Award A1 for the correct numerical value with units. Accept answers in J or kJ.</note>

steps.title: Identify the relevant formula
steps.content:
To calculate the standard Gibbs free energy change ($\Delta G^\ominus$) from an equilibrium constant ($K_c$), we use the thermodynamic relationship found in **Topic R2.3 (How far? The extent of chemical change)**:

$$\Delta G^\ominus = -RT \ln K_c$$

Where:
- $R$ is the universal gas constant ($8.31\ \text{J K}^{-1}\ \text{mol}^{-1}$)
- $T$ is the absolute temperature in Kelvin (K)
- $K_c$ is the equilibrium constant

steps.title: Substitute the given values
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the values provided in the question into the formula. 

$$\begin{aligned} R &= 8.31\ \text{J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1} \\ T &= 298\ \text{K} \\ K_c &= 2.50 \times 10^4 \end{aligned}$$

Substituting these into the equation:
$$\Delta G^\ominus = -(8.31) \times (298) \times \ln(2.50 \times 10^4)$$ 

This substitution step is crucial and typically earns the first mark (M1) in IB exams.

steps.title: Calculate the numerical value
steps.content:
Using a calculator, we evaluate the expression. 

First, calculate the natural logarithm:
$$\ln(25000) \approx 10.127$$

Then, multiply by $R$ and $T$:
$$\Delta G^\ominus \approx -8.31 \times 298 \times 10.127 \approx -25077.39\ \text{J mol}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: In the Run-Matrix menu, enter the calculation:

ti84.instructions: Enter the following expression on the home screen:

desmos.instructions: Type the expression directly into the input bar:

calculator.description: Calculate the Gibbs free energy change using the thermodynamic equation.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Rounding our result to three significant figures (matching the precision of the given data), we get:

$$\Delta G^\ominus = -25100\ \text{J mol}^{-1}$$

Alternatively, you can express this in kilojoules per mole by dividing by $1000$:
$$\Delta G^\ominus = -25.1\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$$

The negative sign indicates that the reaction is spontaneous under standard conditions at this temperature.

parts.content: Comment on the spontaneity of the reaction under standard conditions.
parts.markscheme: The reaction is spontaneous because $\Delta G^\ominus < 0$. <marks>R1</marks>

steps.title: Connect K to Spontaneity
steps.content:
To determine the spontaneity of a reaction, we need to find the sign of the standard Gibbs free energy change, $\Delta G^\ominus$. We can relate this to the equilibrium constant ($K$) using the following equation from the Higher Level syllabus:

$$\Delta G^\ominus = -RT \ln K$$

In this equation, $R$ is the gas constant ($8.31 \text{ J K}^{-1} \text{ mol}^{-1}$) and $T$ is the absolute temperature in Kelvin, both of which are always positive.

steps.title: Evaluate the Logarithm
steps.content:
The question states that the equilibrium constant is $K_c = 2.50 \times 10^4$. Since this value is significantly greater than $1$, its natural logarithm will be positive:

$$\ln(2.50 \times 10^4) \approx 10.13$$

Because $R$, $T$, and $\ln K$ are all positive values, the term $-RT \ln K$ results in a negative value for $\Delta G^\ominus$.

gdc.instructions: Enter ln(25000) into the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Press [ln], enter 25000, and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type 'ln(25000)' into a cell.

calculator.description: Calculating the natural log of the equilibrium constant to check the sign.

steps.title: State the Final Conclusion
steps.content:
In thermodynamics, a reaction is considered spontaneous under standard conditions if $\Delta G^\ominus$ is negative ($\Delta G^\ominus < 0$). 

Since our calculation shows that $\Delta G^\ominus$ is negative when $K > 1$, the reaction is **spontaneous**.

steps.title: Identify the equilibrium constant
steps.content:
To determine the spontaneity of the reaction, we first look at the value of the equilibrium constant provided in the question:

$$K_c = 2.50 \times 10^4$$

steps.title: Analyze the magnitude of K
steps.content:
In chemistry, the **magnitude of $K$** tells us the extent of the reaction at equilibrium:

*   If $K \gg 1$, the reaction favors the products (goes nearly to completion).
*   If $K \ll 1$, the reaction favors the reactants.

Since $2.50 \times 10^4$ is much greater than $1$, the equilibrium lies far to the right, meaning product formation is highly favored.

steps.title: Relate magnitude to spontaneity
steps.content:
A reaction that favors the formation of products at equilibrium is considered **spontaneous** under standard conditions. 

Mathematically, when $K > 1$, the standard Gibbs free energy change ($\Delta G^\ominus$) is negative ($< 0$), which is the thermodynamic definition of spontaneity.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content: The reaction is **spontaneous** because the large value of $K_c$ indicates that products are favored and $\Delta G^\ominus < 0$.

questions.specification:
A student establishes the following equilibrium in a beaker, which results in a deep red solution:

$$\text{Fe}^{3+}\text{(aq)} + \text{SCN}^-\text{(aq)} \rightleftharpoons [\text{Fe(SCN)}]^{2+}\text{(aq)}$$
$$\text{yellow} \quad \quad \text{colorless} \quad \quad \text{red}$$

The student then adds a few drops of aqueous silver nitrate, $\text{AgNO}_3$. The silver ions react with the thiocyanate ions to form a solid white precipitate:

$$\text{Ag}^+\text{(aq)} + \text{SCN}^-\text{(aq)} \rightarrow \text{AgSCN(s)}$$

What is the effect of this addition on the equilibrium position and the appearance of the solution?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.3 How far? The extent of chemical change > R2.3.4 Le Châtelier’s principle
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.3.4 Le Châtelier’s principle
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Adding AgNO3 removes SCN− by precipitating AgSCN(s), decreasing a reactant and shifting the Fe3+ + SCN− ⇌ [FeSCN]2+ equilibrium to the left (Le Châtelier), changing the red colour intensity.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.3 How far? The extent of chemical change > R2.3.1 Dynamic equilibrium
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.3.1 Dynamic equilibrium
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question concerns how a system at dynamic equilibrium responds to a disturbance (removal of SCN−), affecting equilibrium position and observable colour.

options.content: The red color becomes more intense because the equilibrium shifts to the right.
options.markscheme: This is incorrect. Removing a reactant (SCN⁻) causes a shift toward the reactants (the left), not the products (the right).

options.content: The red color fades because the equilibrium shifts to the left.
options.markscheme: According to Le Chatelier's principle, if a reactant is removed from an equilibrium system, the position of equilibrium will shift to the side of the removed substance to counteract the change. Since silver ions precipitate the $\text{SCN}^-$ ions, their concentration decreases, shifting the equilibrium to the left. This reduces the concentration of the red $[\text{Fe(SCN)}]^{2+}$ complex, causing the color to fade.

options.content: The color does not change because silver nitrate acts only as a catalyst.
options.markscheme: This is incorrect. Silver nitrate is not a catalyst; it chemically reacts with one of the species in the equilibrium, altering the concentrations and causing a shift in the position of equilibrium.

options.content: The red color fades because the equilibrium shifts to the right.
options.markscheme: This is a logical contradiction. A shift to the right would increase the concentration of the red complex, $[\text{Fe(SCN)}]^{2+}$, making the color more intense, not paler.

parts.content: Based on the graph, determine the order of the reaction with respect to reactant A. Explain your reasoning.
parts.markscheme:
- State that the graph of rate against concentration is a straight line passing through the origin. <marks>R1</marks>
- Conclude that the rate is directly proportional to concentration, so the reaction is first-order with respect to A. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the graph shape
steps.content:
To determine the order of the reaction, we first look at the relationship shown on the graph. We can see that the plot of the **Rate of reaction** against the **Concentration of A** is a **straight line** that passes through the **origin** $(0,0)$.

This is a classic feature of a first-order reaction in kinetics, which you can study further in **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**.

steps.title: Identify direct proportionality
steps.content:
In mathematics, when a graph is a straight line passing through the origin, it indicates that the variable on the y-axis is **directly proportional** to the variable on the x-axis. 

Mathematically, we can express this as:
$$\text{Rate} \propto [\text{A}]$$

This means if you double the concentration of A, the rate of the reaction will also double.

steps.title: Determine the reaction order
steps.content:
The general rate expression for a reaction involving reactant A is:
$$\text{Rate} = k[\text{A}]^n$$
Where $n$ is the order of the reaction. Since our graph shows that the Rate is directly proportional to $[\text{A}]^1$, we can deduce that:
$$n = 1$$

Therefore, the reaction is **first-order** with respect to reactant A.

steps.title: Final Conclusion and Marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, you must provide both the observation and the conclusion:

1.  **Observation**: The graph is a straight line passing through the origin. (1 mark)
2.  **Conclusion**: The rate is directly proportional to the concentration, meaning the reaction is **first-order** with respect to A. (1 mark)

highlights.text: State that the graph of rate against concentration is a straight line passing through the origin.
Conclude that the rate is directly proportional to concentration, so the reaction is first-order with respect to A.

steps.title: Identify the task
steps.content: The goal is to determine the order of the reaction with respect to A using a comparison of specific data points from the graph. This concept is found in **Reactivity 2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change** (Topic R2.2.10).

highlights.text: determine the order of the reaction with respect to reactant A

steps.title: Select two data points
steps.content:
From the graph, we can pick two easy-to-read points to compare how the rate responds to a change in concentration:

*   **Point 1:** At $[A] = 0.10\text{ mol dm}^{-3}$, the Rate is $0.05\text{ mol dm}^{-3}\text{ s}^{-1}$.
*   **Point 2:** At $[A] = 0.20\text{ mol dm}^{-3}$, the Rate is $0.10\text{ mol dm}^{-3}\text{ s}^{-1}$.

steps.title: Compare the changes
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the factor by which each variable changed between these two points:

*   **Factor of Concentration Change:**
$$\frac{0.20\text{ mol dm}^{-3}}{0.10\text{ mol dm}^{-3}} = 2$$

*   **Factor of Rate Change:**
$$\frac{0.10\text{ mol dm}^{-3}\text{ s}^{-1}}{0.05\text{ mol dm}^{-3}\text{ s}^{-1}} = 2$$

This shows that doubling the concentration $[A]$ also doubles the reaction rate.

gdc.instructions: Divide the new value by the old value for both concentration and rate.

ti84.instructions: Enter 0.20 / 0.10 to find the concentration factor, then enter 0.10 / 0.05 to find the rate factor.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the ratios: 0.2/0.1 and 0.1/0.05.

calculator.description: Calculate the factors of change for both concentration and rate to see their relationship.

steps.title: Determine reaction order
steps.content:
The reaction order $n$ is the power to which the concentration is raised in the rate equation: $\text{Rate} = k[A]^n$. 

Comparing our factors: 
$$2^n = 2$$

This means $n = 1$. This direct proportionality is confirmed because the graph of rate against concentration is a **straight line passing through the origin**.

steps.title: State the answer
steps.content:
The reaction is **first-order** with respect to reactant A. 

Marks are awarded for stating that the graph is a straight line through the origin ($1$ mark) and concluding that the rate is directly proportional to concentration, making it first-order ($1$ mark).

parts.content: Describe one experimental technique that could be used to measure the rate of this reaction. Include the type of data that would be collected.
parts.markscheme:
- State a suitable technique such as colorimetry (spectroscopy) if a species absorbs light, or monitoring gas volume/mass change if a gas is evolved. <marks>M1</marks>
- Describe collecting data such as absorbance, volume of gas, or mass at specific time intervals. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the technique
steps.content: To measure the rate of a reaction like $A \to B$ using the **gas collection method**, we assume that the product $B$ is a gas. A suitable technique is to use a **gas syringe** or a **delivery tube** connected to an inverted measuring cylinder in a water bath to capture the gas as it is evolved.

highlights.text: monitoring gas volume/mass change if a gas is evolved

steps.title: Specify the data collected
steps.content: The specific data you would collect is the **volume of gas produced** at **regular time intervals** (for example, every 30 seconds). By recording how the volume increases over time, you can determine the initial rate of the reaction by calculating the gradient of the volume-time graph at $t=0$.

highlights.text: volume of gas, or mass at specific time intervals

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
In an IB exam, you earn marks as follows:

*   **M1**: For identifying the technique (e.g., using a gas syringe to measure the volume of gas evolved).
*   **A1**: For specifying that measurements of volume are taken at regular time intervals.

steps.title: Identify the Method
steps.content: To measure the rate of the reaction $A \to B$, we can use **colorimetry** (or spectrophotometry). This technique is effective if at least one of the species (reactant $A$ or product $B$) is colored, meaning it absorbs specific wavelengths of light in the visible spectrum.

steps.title: Data Collection Process
steps.content:
The type of data collected is **absorbance**. As the reaction progresses, the concentration of the colored species changes, leading to a proportional change in the amount of light absorbed by the solution. 

To determine the rate, we would measure the **absorbance at regular time intervals** (e.g., every 10 seconds) using a colorimeter or spectrophotometer set to a specific wavelength.

steps.title: Connect to Reaction Rate
steps.content: By plotting a graph of absorbance versus time, the rate of reaction can be determined from the gradient (slope) of the curve. This aligns with the concepts in **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**, where we monitor the change in a physical property over time to track reaction progress.

steps.title: Final Answer Summary
steps.content:
For the 2 marks available:
- **M1:** State the technique: **Colorimetry** (or spectrophotometry).
- **A1:** Describe the data: Measure **absorbance at regular time intervals**.

highlights.text: State a suitable technique such as colorimetry (spectroscopy)
Describe collecting data such as absorbance, volume of gas, or mass at specific time intervals.

steps.title: Select the measurement technique
steps.content: To measure the rate of the reaction $A \to B$, we can use the **Mass Loss Method**. This technique is effective if the product $B$ is a gas that can escape from the reaction vessel, causing the total mass to decrease as the reaction progresses.

steps.title: Set up the apparatus
steps.content: Place the reaction vessel (such as a conical flask) containing reactant $A$ directly onto an **electronic balance**. To prevent liquid splashing out while allowing gas to escape, a piece of cotton wool can be placed in the neck of the flask.

steps.title: Record data over time
steps.content:
The type of data collected is the **mass of the vessel and its contents at specific time intervals** (for example, every 20 seconds). 

This allows you to plot a graph of mass versus time. The gradient of this graph represents the rate of reaction at any given point.

steps.title: Summary of Marks
steps.content:
In an IB exam, you earn marks for:
1.  **M1**: Identifying the technique (monitoring mass change using an electronic balance).
2.  **A1**: Specifying the data collection (recording mass at regular time intervals).

steps.title: Choose the technique
steps.content: To measure the rate of the reaction $A \to B$, we can use the **quenching and titration** method. This technique involves stopping the reaction in small samples at specific times to measure how much reactant is left.

highlights.text: Describe one experimental technique that could be used to measure the rate of this reaction.

steps.title: Take timed aliquots
steps.content: First, we start the reaction and take small, measured samples called **aliquots** at regular, timed intervals (for example, every 60 seconds) using a pipette.

steps.title: Quench the reaction
steps.content:
Each aliquot is immediately **quenched** to stop the reaction. This is usually done by:
- Rapidly cooling the sample in an ice bath.
- Adding a large volume of cold solvent.
- Adding a chemical that removes one of the reactants.

This 'freezes' the concentration of $A$ at the exact moment the sample was taken.

steps.title: Perform the titration
steps.content:
The quenched sample is then **titrated** against a standard solution of known concentration. By using a suitable indicator, we can determine the number of moles of reactant $A$ (or product $B$) in that sample.

In the lab, you would use the formula from your data booklet to find the concentration:
$$\text{concentration} (C) = \frac{\text{moles} (n)}{\text{volume} (V)}$$

gdc.instructions: Divide the product of titrant concentration and volume by the aliquot volume.

ti84.instructions: Calculate moles (n = C*V) then divide by sample volume (C = n/V).

desmos.instructions: Calculate the final concentration based on the titration values.

calculator.description: Calculate the concentration of a 10.0 cm³ sample that required 15.0 cm³ of a 0.100 mol dm⁻³ titrant.

steps.title: Identify data collected
steps.content:
The data collected through this method is the **concentration of $A$ (or $B$) at specific time intervals**. 

This data allows us to plot a concentration-time graph, where the gradient at any point gives the rate of reaction at that moment.

highlights.text: Include the type of data that would be collected.

parts.content:
Using the graph, determine the rate constant ($k$) for this reaction, assuming the reaction follows the rate law:

$$
\text{Rate} = k[\text{A}]^n
$$
parts.markscheme:
- Identify that for a first-order reaction, $\text{Rate} = k[\text{A}]$.
- Substitute values from a point on the graph into the expression $k = \frac{\text{Rate}}{[\text{A}]}$, e.g., $[\text{A}] = 0.20\text{ mol\,dm}^{-3}$ and $\text{Rate} = 1.00\text{ mol\,dm}^{-3}\,\text{s}^{-1}$. <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the rate constant: $k = \frac{1.00\text{ mol\,dm}^{-3}\,\text{s}^{-1}}{0.20\text{ mol\,dm}^{-3}} = 5.0\text{ s}^{-1}$. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

methods.label: Substitution of a Single Data Point

steps.title: Determine the reaction order
steps.content:
First, we look at the shape of the graph. The graph of **Rate of reaction** versus **Concentration of A** is a straight line passing through the origin. This indicates that the rate is directly proportional to the concentration:

$$\text{Rate} \propto [\text{A}]$$

This means the reaction is **first-order** with respect to A, so the exponent $n$ in the rate law is $1$. The rate equation is:

$$\text{Rate} = k[\text{A}]$$

This concept is covered in **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**.

steps.title: Select a data point
steps.content:
To find the rate constant $k$, we can pick any single point along the line of best fit from the graph. 

Let's choose a point where the values are easy to read. For example, when the concentration $[\text{A}]$ is $0.20\text{ mol dm}^{-3}$, the rate is $1.00\text{ mol dm}^{-3} \text{s}^{-1}$.

*   $[\text{A}] = 0.20\text{ mol dm}^{-3}$
*   $\text{Rate} = 1.00\text{ mol dm}^{-3} \text{s}^{-1}$

steps.title: Calculate the rate constant
steps.content:
Now, we substitute these values into our rate equation and solve for $k$:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Rate} &= k[\text{A}] \\ 1.00 &= k(0.20) \\ k &= \frac{1.00}{0.20} \end{aligned}$$

Using a calculator, we find:

$$k = 5.0$$

gdc.instructions: Input 1.00 / 0.20 into the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter 1.00 divided by 0.20 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 1.00 / 0.20 in the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the value of the rate constant k.

steps.title: Determine final units
steps.content:
In IB Chemistry, units are essential for the rate constant. For a first-order reaction, the units are calculated as:

$$k = \frac{\text{Rate}}{[\text{A}]} = \frac{\text{mol dm}^{-3} \text{ s}^{-1}}{\text{mol dm}^{-3}} = \text{s}^{-1}$$

Thus, the final answer is:

$$k = 5.0\text{ s}^{-1}$$

This would earn you both marks: one for the correct substitution (M1) and one for the final value with units (A1).

steps.title: Identify the reaction order
steps.content:
Looking at the graph, we see a straight line passing through the origin. This indicates that the rate of reaction is directly proportional to the concentration of reactant A. 

In the rate law expression:
$$\text{Rate} = k[\text{A}]^n$$

When the graph of Rate vs. Concentration is linear, the exponent $n$ must be 1. This means we are dealing with a **first-order reaction**. Prerequisite knowledge of rate equations can be found in **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**.

steps.title: Relate k to the gradient
steps.content:
For a first-order reaction, the rate equation is:
$$\text{Rate} = k[\text{A}]$$

If we compare this to the equation for a straight line, $y = mx + c$:
- $y$ is the **Rate**
- $x$ is the concentration **[A]**
- $m$ (the gradient) is the rate constant **$k$**
- $c$ (the y-intercept) is 0

Therefore, to find the rate constant $k$, we just need to calculate the gradient (slope) of the line.

steps.title: Select points from graph
steps.content:
To calculate the gradient, we pick two points on the line. Since the line passes through the origin $(0,0)$, that's an easy first point. Let's pick a clear point from the data table or graph for the second one:

- **Point 1:** $([\text{A}]_1, \text{Rate}_1) = (0, 0)$
- **Point 2:** $([\text{A}]_2, \text{Rate}_2) = (0.20, 1.00)$

highlights.text: [A] = 0.20 mol dm^{-3} and Rate = 1.00 mol dm^{-3} s^{-1}

steps.title: Calculate the rate constant
steps.content:
Now we use the gradient formula:
$$k = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{\text{Rate}_2 - \text{Rate}_1}{[\text{A}]_2 - [\text{A}]_1}$$

Substituting our values:
$$k = \frac{1.00 - 0}{0.20 - 0} = \frac{1.00}{0.20}$$

$$k = 5.0\text{ s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Divide the rate by the concentration.

ti84.instructions: Enter the division on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Perform the division in a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the gradient of the line to find the rate constant k.

steps.title: Final answer and units
steps.content:
The rate constant is $5.0\text{ s}^{-1}$. 

Remember to check your units! For a first-order reaction, the units of $k$ are:
$$\text{units of } k = \frac{\text{mol\,dm}^{-3}\text{\,s}^{-1}}{\text{mol\,dm}^{-3}} = \text{s}^{-1}$$

parts.content: If the initial concentration of A is $0.25\text{ mol\,dm}^{-3}$, calculate the time required for the concentration of A to decrease to $0.125\text{ mol\,dm}^{-3}$. Assume the reaction is first-order and use the rate constant determined in part 3.
parts.markscheme:
- State the integrated rate law for a first-order reaction, $\ln \frac{[\text{A}]_0}{[\text{A}]} = kt$, or recognize the interval represents one half-life, $t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k}$. <marks>M1</marks>
- Substitute the given values: $\ln \frac{0.25}{0.125} = 5.0 \times t$ or $t = \frac{\ln 2}{5.0}$. <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the final time: $t \approx 0.139\text{ s}$. <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify the known values
steps.content:
To solve this problem using the integrated rate law approach, we first need to extract the given values from the question:

*   **Initial concentration** $([A]_0)$: $0.25 \text{ mol\,dm}^{-3}$
*   **Final concentration** $([A]_t)$: $0.125 \text{ mol\,dm}^{-3}$
*   **Rate constant** $(k)$: $5.0 \text{ s}^{-1}$ (this was determined from the slope of the Rate vs. Concentration graph in the previous part of the question).

The question also confirms the reaction is **first-order**.

highlights.text: initial concentration of A is 0.25 mol dm^-3

steps.title: Apply the Integrated Rate Law
steps.content:
For a first-order reaction, the relationship between concentration and time is given by the integrated rate law formula:

$$\ln \frac{[A]_0}{[A]_t} = kt$$

Alternatively, you might see it written as:
$$\ln [A]_t = -kt + \ln [A]_0$$

Since we want to find the time ($t$), we will use the first version to isolate $t$ more easily. This formula is a core part of **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change** in the IB HL Chemistry syllabus.

steps.title: Substitute and rearrange
steps.content:
Now, we substitute our known values into the equation:

$$\ln \left( \frac{0.25}{0.125} \right) = 5.0 \times t$$

First, simplify the fraction inside the natural log:
$$\frac{0.25}{0.125} = 2$$

So the equation becomes:
$$\ln(2) = 5.0 \times t$$

To solve for $t$, divide both sides by the rate constant $(k)$:
$$t = \frac{\ln(2)}{5.0}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter ln(0.25/0.125)/5.0 in the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Press [ln], enter (0.25/0.125), press [)], then press [/] and enter 5.0. Press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type ln(0.25/0.125)/5.0 into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the time using the natural logarithm and the rate constant.

steps.title: Final calculation
steps.content:
Using a calculator to find the value:

$$t = \frac{0.6931...}{5.0}$$
$$t \approx 0.139 \text{ s}$$

Notice that the concentration $0.125$ is exactly half of $0.25$. This means the time we just calculated is actually the **half-life** ($t_{1/2}$) of the reaction! For first-order reactions, the half-life is constant regardless of the starting concentration.

steps.title: Analyze the concentration change
steps.content:
First, let's look at the initial and final concentrations given in the problem. 

We start with $[\text{A}]_0 = 0.25\text{ mol\,dm}^{-3}$ and end with $[\text{A}] = 0.125\text{ mol\,dm}^{-3}$. 

You might notice that $0.125$ is exactly half of $0.25$:
$$\frac{0.125}{0.25} = 0.5$$

In chemical kinetics, the time it takes for a concentration to decrease by exactly half is known as the **half-life** ($t_{1/2}$).

highlights.text: initial concentration of A is $0.25\text{ mol\,dm}^{-3}$

steps.title: Select the half-life formula
steps.content:
Since the question specifies the reaction is **first-order**, we can use the specific relationship between the rate constant ($k$) and the half-life ($t_{1/2}$). 

For any first-order reaction, the half-life is constant and is calculated using:

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k}$$

This relationship is derived from the integrated rate law covered in **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**.

steps.title: Substitute the rate constant
steps.content:
From part 3 of this problem, we know the rate constant is $k = 5.0\text{ s}^{-1}$. Now, we just need to plug this value into our half-life equation:

$$t = \frac{\ln 2}{5.0}$$

highlights.text: use the rate constant determined in part 3

steps.title: Calculate the final time
steps.content:
Using a calculator to find the value:

$$t = \frac{0.693}{5.0} \approx 0.139\text{ s}$$

This is the time required for the concentration to drop by half (one half-life).

gdc.instructions: Enter ln(2) divided by 5.0 in the calculation menu.

desmos.instructions: Type 'ln(2)/5' into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the half-life for a first-order reaction.

questions.specification:
The synthesis of ammonia from nitrogen and hydrogen gas is represented by the following equation:

$$\mathrm{N}_2(g) + 3\mathrm{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\mathrm{NH}_3(g)$$

The table shows the concentrations of the species present in an equilibrium mixture at a constant temperature.

| Species | Equilibrium concentration / $$\text{mol dm}^{-3}$$ |
| :--- | :--- |
| $$\mathrm{N}_2(g)$$ | $$0.20$$ |
| $$\mathrm{H}_2(g)$$ | $$0.10$$ |
| $$\mathrm{NH}_3(g)$$ | $$0.40$$ |

What is the value of the equilibrium constant, $$K_c$$, at this temperature?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.3 How far? The extent of chemical change > R2.3.2 The equilibrium law and constant
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.3.2 The equilibrium law and constant
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for calculating the equilibrium constant Kc from given equilibrium concentrations using the equilibrium expression for N2 + 3H2 ⇌ 2NH3.

questions.specification:
Consider the equilibrium reaction:

$2\mathrm{NO_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{N_2O_4}(g)$

At the start of the reaction, $[\mathrm{NO_2}] = 0.60\ \mathrm{mol\,dm^{-3}}$. At equilibrium, the concentration of $\mathrm{N_2O_4}$ is $0.20\ \mathrm{mol\,dm^{-3}}$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.3 How far? The extent of chemical change > R2.3.2 The equilibrium law and constant
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.3.2 The equilibrium law and constant
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (b) and (c) ask for the Kc expression and calculation from equilibrium concentrations, which directly tests the equilibrium law and equilibrium constant.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.3 How far? The extent of chemical change > R2.3.4 Le Châtelier’s principle
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.3.4 Le Châtelier’s principle
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (d) asks how halving volume affects the equilibrium position with justification, which is an application of Le Châtelier’s principle (pressure/volume changes in gaseous equilibria).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.3 How far? The extent of chemical change > R2.3.6 Quantifying equilibrium concentrations (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.3.6 Quantifying equilibrium concentrations (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) requires determining equilibrium [NO2] from initial concentration and equilibrium [N2O4] using stoichiometry/ICE reasoning, i.e., quantifying equilibrium concentrations.

parts.content: Calculate the equilibrium concentration of $\mathrm{NO_2}$.
parts.markscheme:
- $2\mathrm{NO_2} \to \mathrm{N_2O_4}$ indicates 1 mol of $\mathrm{N_2O_4}$ forms from 2 mol $\mathrm{NO_2}$
- $\Delta[\mathrm{NO_2}] = 2 \times 0.20 = 0.40$
- $[\mathrm{NO_2}] = 0.60 - 0.40 = 0.20\ \mathrm{mol\,dm^{-3}}$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Write the expression for the equilibrium constant $K_c$.
parts.markscheme:
$K_c = \dfrac{[\mathrm{N_2O_4}]}{[\mathrm{NO_2}]^2}$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify products and reactants
steps.content:
To write the $K_c$ expression, we first need to identify the components of our chemical equation. This reaction involves the conversion between nitrogen dioxide and dinitrogen tetroxide:

$$2\mathrm{NO_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{N_2O_4}(g)$$

*   **Reactant:** $\mathrm{NO_2}$ (with a stoichiometric coefficient of **2**)
*   **Product:** $\mathrm{N_2O_4}$ (with a stoichiometric coefficient of **1**)

This concept is a core part of **Topic R2.3: How far? The extent of chemical change**.

steps.title: Apply the Equilibrium Law
steps.content:
The equilibrium law states that the equilibrium constant $K_c$ is the ratio of the product of the concentrations of the products to the product of the concentrations of the reactants. 

Each concentration term is raised to the power of its stoichiometric coefficient from the balanced equation:

$$K_c = \frac{[\text{Products}]^y}{[\text{Reactants}]^x}$$

In this specific case:
*   The product $[\mathrm{N_2O_4}]$ is raised to the power of **1**.
*   The reactant $[\mathrm{NO_2}]$ is raised to the power of **2**.

steps.title: Formulate the final expression
steps.content:
Combining these parts, we place the product in the numerator and the reactant in the denominator to get the final expression:

$$K_c = \frac{[\mathrm{N_2O_4}]}{[\mathrm{NO_2}]^2}$$

Remember that square brackets $[\ ]$ denote concentration in $\text{mol\,dm}^{-3}$ at equilibrium. This expression is worth **1 mark** in your exam.

parts.content: Calculate the value of $K_c$.
parts.markscheme:
$K_c = \dfrac{0.20}{(0.20)^2} = \dfrac{0.20}{0.04} = 5.0$

<note>
<marks>1 mark</marks> for substitution,
<marks>1 mark</marks> for final answer
</note>

steps.title: Define the Equilibrium Expression
steps.content:
To calculate the equilibrium constant $K_c$, we first need to write the equilibrium law expression for the given reaction:

$$2\mathrm{NO_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{N_2O_4}(g)$$

The expression is the ratio of product concentrations to reactant concentrations, each raised to the power of their stoichiometric coefficients:

$$K_c = \frac{[\mathrm{N_2O_4}]}{[\mathrm{NO_2}]^2}$$

This concept is covered in **Topic R2.3: How far? The extent of chemical change**.

steps.title: Set up the ICE Table
steps.content:
An **ICE table** (Initial, Change, Equilibrium) helps us track how the concentrations change as the system reaches equilibrium. We start with the values given in the question.

$$\begin{array}{|l|c|c|} \hline \text{Concentration } (\mathrm{mol\,dm^{-3}}) & 2\mathrm{NO_2} & \mathrm{N_2O_4} \\ \hline \text{Initial (I)} & 0.60 & 0.00 \\ \hline \text{Change (C)} & -2x & +x \\ \hline \text{Equilibrium (E)} & 0.60 - 2x & x \\ \hline \end{array}$$

Note that the change follows the stoichiometry: for every $x$ moles of $\mathrm{N_2O_4}$ produced, $2x$ moles of $\mathrm{NO_2}$ are consumed.

steps.title: Determine the Change (x)
steps.content:
The question provides the equilibrium concentration of the product, $\mathrm{N_2O_4}$. According to our ICE table, the equilibrium concentration of $\mathrm{N_2O_4}$ is equal to $x$.

$$x = 0.20\ \mathrm{mol\,dm^{-3}}$$

steps.title: Calculate Equilibrium [NO2]
steps.content:
Now we can find the equilibrium concentration of $\mathrm{NO_2}$ by substituting the value of $x$ back into our expression from the ICE table:

$$\begin{aligned} [\mathrm{NO_2}]_{eq} &= 0.60 - 2x \\ &= 0.60 - 2(0.20) \\ &= 0.60 - 0.40 \\ &= 0.20\ \mathrm{mol\,dm^{-3}} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 0.60 - 2 * 0.20 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the equilibrium concentration of NO2.

steps.title: Calculate the Kc Value
steps.content:
Finally, substitute the equilibrium concentrations into the $K_c$ expression. In an IB exam, showing this substitution step correctly is worth 1 mark.

$$K_c = \frac{0.20}{(0.20)^2}$$

$$\begin{aligned} K_c &= \frac{0.20}{0.040} \\ K_c &= 5.0 \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 0.20 / (0.20^2) and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the final Kc value.

parts.content: State how the position of equilibrium would change if the volume of the container were halved. Justify your answer.
parts.markscheme:
- Volume halved $\to$ pressure increases
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- Shifts toward side with fewer gas molecules (products) to reduce pressure
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the pressure change
steps.content:
First, we need to understand how changing the volume affects the conditions inside the container. According to the ideal gas law ($$PV = nRT$$), pressure is inversely proportional to volume. 

Therefore, if the **volume of the container is halved**, the total **pressure increases**. This change in pressure is the "stress" applied to the equilibrium system.

highlights.text: volume of the container were halved

steps.title: Compare gaseous moles
steps.content:
Next, we examine the balanced chemical equation to see how many moles of gas are on each side:

- **Reactant side**: $2$ moles of gas ($2\mathrm{NO_2}$)
- **Product side**: $1$ mole of gas ($1\mathrm{N_2O_4}$)

We can see that the product side has fewer moles of gas molecules than the reactant side.

steps.title: Apply Le Chatelier's Principle
steps.content:
**Le Chatelier’s Principle** states that if a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it will shift its position to oppose the change. 

Since the pressure has increased, the equilibrium will **shift to the side with fewer moles of gas** to reduce the total number of molecules and thus decrease the pressure. This means the equilibrium position shifts to the **right** (towards the products).

steps.title: Relate volume to concentration
steps.content:
To understand how the equilibrium shifts, we first need to see how changing the volume affects the concentrations. From **Topic S1.4: Molar concentration**, we know that concentration ($C$) is inversely proportional to volume ($V$):

$$C = \frac{n}{V}$$

If the **volume of the container were halved**, the concentration of every gas in the mixture will **double**.

highlights.text: volume of the container were halved

steps.title: Write the equilibrium expression
steps.content:
Using the balanced equation provided in the question, we can write the expression for the equilibrium constant, $K_c$. This falls under **Topic R2.3: The equilibrium law**.

$$2\mathrm{NO_2}(g) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{N_2O_4}(g)$$

$$K_c = \frac{[\mathrm{N_2O_4}]_{eq}}{[\mathrm{NO_2}]_{eq}^2}$$

steps.title: Calculate the Reaction Quotient
steps.content:
Now, let's find the reaction quotient ($Q$) immediately after the volume is halved. Since all concentrations have doubled, we substitute $2 \times [\text{concentration}]$ into our expression:

$$\begin{aligned} Q &= \frac{2[\mathrm{N_2O_4}]_{eq}}{(2[\mathrm{NO_2}]_{eq})^2} \\ Q &= \frac{2[\mathrm{N_2O_4}]_{eq}}{4[\mathrm{NO_2}]_{eq}^2} \\ Q &= 0.5 K_c \end{aligned}$$

Because $Q$ is now less than $K_c$ ($Q < K_c$), the system is no longer at equilibrium.

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the ratio of the concentration changes: 2 / 2^2.

ti84.instructions: Divide the multiplier for the numerator (2) by the square of the multiplier for the denominator (2^2).

desmos.instructions: Enter the fraction of the multipliers to see the effect on the quotient.

calculator.description: Verification that doubling concentrations in a 2:1 gas reaction results in Q being half of Kc.

steps.title: Determine the equilibrium shift
steps.content:
Since $Q < K_c$, the reaction must shift to **increase the value of $Q$** until it equals $K_c$ again. This means the reaction will favor the formation of more products.

**The position of equilibrium shifts to the right (toward the products).**

This makes sense because the right side has **fewer gas molecules** (1 mole of $\mathrm{N_2O_4}$ vs 2 moles of $\mathrm{NO_2}$), which helps reduce the increased pressure caused by halving the volume.
