parts.content: Predict and explain the effect of adding more $\text{O}_2(\text{g})$ on the position of equilibrium.
parts.markscheme:
- the equilibrium position shifts to the right / towards the products <marks>A1</marks>
- the system acts to reduce the concentration of the added reactant / $\text{O}_2$ <marks>R1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the change
steps.content:
The question asks us to predict what happens when we increase the amount of $\text{O}_2(\text{g})$. In the given chemical equation:

$$2\text{SO}_2(\text{g}) + \text{O}_2(\text{g}) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{SO}_3(\text{g})$$

$\text{O}_2$ is a **reactant**. Increasing its concentration creates a stress on the system that was previously at equilibrium.

steps.title: Apply Le Chatelier's Principle
steps.content:
According to **Le Chatelier's Principle**, if a system at equilibrium is disturbed by a change in concentration, the system will shift its equilibrium position to **counteract** or oppose that change.

Since we added $\text{O}_2$, the system responds by trying to **reduce the concentration** of the added $\text{O}_2$.

steps.title: Determine the shift direction
steps.content:
To reduce the concentration of $\text{O}_2$, the system must consume it. The forward reaction (moving from left to right) uses up the reactants to produce the product, $\text{SO}_3$.

Therefore, the equilibrium position **shifts to the right** (towards the products).

steps.title: Summarize for marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on an IB exam, you must provide both the prediction and the justification:

1.  **Prediction:** The equilibrium position shifts to the right (or towards the products).
2.  **Explanation:** The system acts to reduce the concentration of the added reactant ($\text{O}_2$).

steps.title: State the Equilibrium Expression
steps.content:
To understand the effect of adding a reactant, we first write the equilibrium constant expression ($K_c$) for the given balanced equation:

$$2\text{SO}_2(\text{g}) + \text{O}_2(\text{g}) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{SO}_3(\text{g})$$

Based on the Equilibrium Law, the expression is:

$$K_c = \frac{[\text{SO}_3]^2}{[\text{SO}_2]^2 [\text{O}_2]}$$

steps.title: Analyze the Reaction Quotient
steps.content:
When we add more $\text{O}_2(\text{g})$, the concentration of oxygen increases. Since $\text{O}_2$ is in the **denominator** of our quotient, increasing its concentration increases the value of the denominator.

Immediately after the addition, the reaction is no longer at equilibrium. We describe its current state using the reaction quotient ($Q_c$):

steps.title: Compare Qc and Kc
steps.content:
Since the denominator of the fraction has increased, the overall value of the fraction decreases. This means that at the moment of addition:

$$Q_c < K_c$$

To re-establish equilibrium, the system must increase the value of $Q_c$ until it once again equals $K_c$.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the ratio with an increased denominator value.

ti84.instructions: Enter 1 / (1 * 2) and observe the result is less than 1.

desmos.instructions: Compare 1 to 0.5.

calculator.description: Suppose the concentrations are all 1. Then Kc = 1. If we increase the denominator [O2] to 2, Qc becomes 0.5. Since 0.5 < 1, Qc is less than Kc.

steps.title: Determine the Equilibrium Shift
steps.content:
To increase the value of $Q_c$, the reaction must proceed in a direction that increases the numerator (products) and decreases the denominator (reactants). 

Therefore, the equilibrium position shifts to the **right** (towards the products). This acts to reduce the concentration of the added reactant, $\text{O}_2$.

highlights.text: the equilibrium position shifts to the right / towards the products
the system acts to reduce the concentration of the added reactant / $\text{O}_2$

parts.content: State and explain the effect of a catalyst on the position of equilibrium and the value of the equilibrium constant, $K_{\text{c}}$.
parts.markscheme:
- there is no effect on the position of equilibrium AND no effect on the value of $K_{\text{c}}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- the catalyst increases the rate of both forward and reverse reactions equally <marks>R1</marks>

steps.title: State the effects
steps.content: For any chemical system at equilibrium, including the reaction $2\text{SO}_2(\text{g}) + \text{O}_2(\text{g}) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{SO}_3(\text{g})$, the addition of a catalyst has **no effect** on the position of equilibrium and **no effect** on the value of the equilibrium constant, $K_{\text{c}}$.

steps.title: Explain using kinetic rates
steps.content:
From a kinetic perspective, a catalyst provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy ($E_{\text{a}}$). This can be seen in the Arrhenius equation:

$$k=A e^{\frac{-E_{\text{a}}}{RT}}$$

By lowering $E_{\text{a}}$, the rate constant ($k$) increases. Crucially, a catalyst lowers the activation energy for **both** the forward and the reverse reactions by the same amount. Therefore, the catalyst increases the rate of the forward reaction and the rate of the reverse reaction **equally**.

steps.title: Conclusion on equilibrium
steps.content:
Because both the forward and reverse rates are increased by the same factor, the system reaches the state of equilibrium faster. However, since neither rate is favored over the other, the relative concentrations of reactants and products at equilibrium do not change. 

This explains why:
1. The **position of equilibrium** remains the same.
2. The **equilibrium constant ($K_{\text{c}}$)** remains unchanged, as it is only affected by changes in temperature.

steps.title: State the overall effect
steps.content:
When dealing with a catalyst in an equilibrium system like this one, the first thing to remember is that it does not change where the equilibrium "sits" or the numerical value of the constant. 

There is **no effect** on the **position of equilibrium** and **no effect** on the value of the **equilibrium constant**, $K_{\text{c}}$.

steps.title: Alternative reaction pathway
steps.content:
To explain *why* there's no change, we look at how a catalyst functions. A catalyst works by providing an **alternative reaction pathway**. 

This new route has a **lower activation energy** ($E_{\text{a}}$) compared to the original uncatalyzed pathway. This is a core concept from **Topic R2.2: Catalysts**.

steps.title: Impact on both directions
steps.content:
The key to the equilibrium argument is that this alternative pathway with lower $E_{\text{a}}$ applies to **both the forward and the reverse reactions**. 

If the activation energy for the forward reaction decreases, the activation energy for the reverse reaction decreases by exactly the same amount. This is because both reactions now pass through the same lower-energy transition state.

steps.title: Rates increase equally
steps.content:
Because the activation energy is lowered for both directions, the catalyst **increases the rate of both the forward and reverse reactions equally**.

Mathematically, we can see this relation in the Arrhenius equation from your data booklet:

$$k = A e^{\frac{-E_{\text{a}}}{RT}}$$

As $E_{\text{a}}$ decreases, the rate constant $k$ increases. Since both $k_{\text{forward}}$ and $k_{\text{reverse}}$ increase by the same factor, the system reaches equilibrium faster, but the final ratio of products to reactants remains exactly the same.

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To ensure you get full marks on an IB exam:
1.  State that there is **no change** to the equilibrium position or $K_{\text{c}}$ value.
2.  Explain that the catalyst **increases the rate of both forward and reverse reactions equally** by providing a pathway with lower activation energy.

steps.title: State the observed effect
steps.content:
In IB Chemistry, it is a fundamental rule that adding a catalyst to a system at equilibrium has **no effect** on the position of equilibrium and **no effect** on the value of the equilibrium constant, $K_{\text{c}}$.

This is a common "trick" question—while a catalyst changes how *fast* you get to equilibrium, it doesn't change *where* that equilibrium is.

steps.title: Apply temperature dependence logic
steps.content:
To explain why $K_{\text{c}}$ remains unchanged, we look at what variables actually affect it. As covered in **Topic R2.3.3**, the equilibrium constant $K_{\text{c}}$ is temperature-dependent only. 

Because the addition of a catalyst does not change the temperature of the system, the value of $K_{\text{c}}$ must remain constant. Since $K_{\text{c}}$ represents the fixed ratio of products to reactants at a specific temperature, if $K_{\text{c}}$ doesn't change, the **position of equilibrium** remains unaffected.

steps.title: Explain using reaction rates
steps.content:
To secure the second mark according to the markscheme, we must also explain what is happening to the individual reaction rates. 

A catalyst works by providing an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy ($E_a$). This lowers the barrier for **both** the forward and the reverse reactions equally. 

$$\text{Rate}_{\text{forward}} \uparrow \text{ and } \text{Rate}_{\text{reverse}} \uparrow \text{ by the same factor}$$

Since both directions are sped up to the same extent, the system maintains the same balance of concentrations, meaning the equilibrium position does not shift.

options.content: The equilibrium constant $K$ is much less than 1, and the reaction mixture contains mostly reactants.
options.markscheme: A negative $\Delta G^\circ$ corresponds to an equilibrium constant $K > 1$. If $K$ were much less than 1, $\Delta G^\circ$ would be a large positive value.

options.content: The equilibrium constant $K$ is much greater than 1, and the reaction has proceeded almost to completion.
options.markscheme: The relationship between the standard Gibbs free energy change and the equilibrium constant is $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$. A large negative value for $\Delta G^\circ$ ($-120\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$) results in a very large positive value for $K$ ($K = e^{-\Delta G^\circ / RT}$). This indicates that the equilibrium position lies far to the right, favoring product formation.

options.content: The equilibrium constant $K$ is equal to 1, and the concentrations of reactants and products are equal.
options.markscheme: For $K$ to be 1, the standard Gibbs free energy change ($\Delta G^\circ$) must be zero. Since $\Delta G^\circ = -120\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$, the system is heavily biased toward the products.

options.content: The equilibrium constant $K$ is equal to 0, and no products are formed from the reactants.
options.markscheme: An equilibrium constant of 0 is mathematically impossible for a reversible reaction as it would imply $\ln K$ approaches negative infinity, requiring an infinitely large positive $\Delta G^\circ$.

questions.specification:
The following thermochemical equation represents the production of hydrogen gas from steam and solid carbon:

$$\text{C}(\text{s}) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(\text{g}) \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}(\text{g}) + \text{H}_2(\text{g}) \quad \Delta H = +131 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

Which row in the table identifies the conditions of temperature and pressure that would result in the highest equilibrium yield of hydrogen gas?

| Row | Temperature | Pressure |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A | Decreased | Increased |
| B | Increased | Decreased |
| C | Decreased | Decreased |
| D | Increased | Increased |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.3 How far? The extent of chemical change > R2.3.4 Le Châtelier’s principle
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.3.4 Le Châtelier’s principle
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Determining how changes in temperature (endothermic forward reaction) and pressure shift an equilibrium position to maximize H2 yield is a direct application of Le Châtelier’s principle.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.3 How far? The extent of chemical change > R2.3.3 Magnitude of K and temperature dependence
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.3.3 Magnitude of K and temperature dependence
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Temperature is varied and ΔH is given, so the question relies on the temperature dependence of equilibrium (K changes with temperature for endo/exothermic reactions).

options.markscheme: Decreasing temperature favors the exothermic (reverse) direction, and increasing pressure favors the side with fewer moles of gas (the left side has 1 mole of gas, the right has 2), both of which decrease the yield of hydrogen.

The reaction is endothermic ($+\Delta H$), so increasing the temperature shifts the equilibrium to the right to favor product formation. There is 1 mole of gas on the reactant side (carbon is solid) and 2 moles of gas on the product side; therefore, decreasing the pressure shifts the equilibrium toward the side with more moles of gas (the products).

While decreasing the pressure favors the side with more moles of gas (the products), decreasing the temperature favors the exothermic (reverse) reaction for an endothermic process, preventing a maximum yield.

While increasing the temperature favors the endothermic forward reaction, increasing the pressure shifts the equilibrium toward the side with fewer moles of gas (the reactant side), which would reduce the yield of hydrogen gas.

The reaction below occurs in aqueous solution:

$$\mathrm{CH_3COOH(aq)} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CH_3COO}^-(\mathrm{aq}) + \mathrm{H}^+(\mathrm{aq})$$

parts.content: Write the equilibrium expression for $K_c$.
parts.markscheme:
- Correct expression for $K_c$: $K_c = \dfrac{[\mathrm{CH_3COO}^-][\mathrm{H}^+]}{[\mathrm{CH_3COOH}]}$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify reactants and products
steps.content:
First, we look at the provided chemical equation to identify which species are the reactants and which are the products. 

In this equilibrium system:
- **Reactant:** $\mathrm{CH_3COOH(aq)}$ (Ethanoic acid)
- **Products:** $\mathrm{CH_3COO}^-(\mathrm{aq})$ (Ethanoate ion) and $\mathrm{H}^+(\mathrm{aq})$ (Hydrogen ion)

This concept is a core part of **Topic R2.3: How far? The extent of chemical change**.

steps.title: Apply the Law of Mass Action
steps.content:
To write the equilibrium constant expression ($K_c$), we follow the **law of mass action**. This states that the equilibrium constant is equal to the product of the concentrations of the products divided by the product of the concentrations of the reactants.

Key rules to remember:
1. Use square brackets $[\]$ to represent molar concentration.
2. Products are placed in the **numerator** (top).
3. Reactants are placed in the **denominator** (bottom).
4. Each concentration is raised to the power of its stoichiometric coefficient (in this reaction, all coefficients are 1).

steps.title: Construct the $K_c$ expression
steps.content:
Now, we substitute our specific chemical species into the general formula:

$$K_c = \frac{[\text{products}]}{[\text{reactants}]}$$

Placing our products on top and our reactant on the bottom, we get:

$$K_c = \frac{[\mathrm{CH_3COO}^-][\mathrm{H}^+]}{[\mathrm{CH_3COOH}]}$$

This format is required to earn the mark in an IB exam.

parts.content: Predict and explain the effect of adding $\mathrm{NaOH}$ to the mixture.
parts.markscheme:
- $\mathrm{OH}^-$ reacts with $\mathrm{H}^+$ <marks>A1</marks>
- System shifts to the right to replace $\mathrm{H}^+$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Forms more $\mathrm{CH_3COO}^-$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify the species added
steps.content: When sodium hydroxide ($\mathrm{NaOH}$) is added to the aqueous solution, it dissociates completely into sodium ions ($\mathrm{Na}^{+}$) and hydroxide ions ($\mathrm{OH}^{-}$). The hydroxide ions are highly reactive in this system.

steps.title: Neutralization of hydrogen ions
steps.content:
The hydroxide ions ($\mathrm{OH}^{-}$) react with the hydrogen ions ($\mathrm{H}^{+}$) present in the equilibrium mixture to form water:

$$\mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) + \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$$

This process effectively removes $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ ions from the equilibrium system.

steps.title: Apply Le Chatelier's Principle
steps.content:
According to **Le Chatelier’s Principle** (covered in **Topic R2.3**), if a change is imposed on a system at equilibrium, the system will shift to oppose that change. 

Because the concentration of a product ($\mathrm{H}^{+}$) has decreased, the equilibrium will shift to the **right** (towards the products) to replace the lost $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ ions.

steps.title: Predict the final effect
steps.content:
As the equilibrium shifts to the right, more acetic acid ($\mathrm{CH_{3}COOH}$) dissociates. This leads to an increase in the concentration of the ethanoate ion ($\mathrm{CH_{3}COO}^{-}$).

In summary:
- $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ reacts with $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ ions.
- The equilibrium shifts to the **right**.
- More **$\mathrm{CH_{3}COO}^{-}$** is formed.

steps.title: Identify the chemical interaction
steps.content:
When $\mathrm{NaOH}$ is added to the solution, it dissociates completely to release hydroxide ions ($\mathrm{OH}^-$). These $\mathrm{OH}^-$ ions will react with the hydrogen ions ($\mathrm{H}^+$) present in the equilibrium mixture to form water:

$$\mathrm{H}^+(\mathrm{aq}) + \mathrm{OH}^-(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow \mathrm{H_2O}(\mathrm{l})$$

This neutralization reaction effectively removes $\mathrm{H}^+$ ions from the equilibrium system, causing their concentration, $[\mathrm{H}^+]$, to decrease.

steps.title: Analyze the Reaction Quotient
steps.content:
To understand how the system responds, we look at the reaction quotient ($Q$). The expression for $Q$ (which has the same form as the equilibrium constant $K_c$) is:

$$Q = \frac{[\mathrm{CH_3COO}^-][\mathrm{H}^+]}{[\mathrm{CH_3COOH}]}$$

Because the concentration of the product $\mathrm{H}^+$ has decreased due to neutralization, the numerator of this fraction becomes smaller. Consequently, the value of the reaction quotient $Q$ becomes less than the equilibrium constant $K_c$ ($Q < K_c$).

steps.title: Predict the equilibrium shift
steps.content:
According to the principles of chemical equilibrium, if $Q < K_c$, the system is no longer at equilibrium and will shift to restore it. 

To make $Q$ equal to $K_c$ again, the system must increase the concentration of the products (the numerator). Therefore, the equilibrium shifts to the **right** (the forward direction). 

As a result, more $\mathrm{CH_3COOH}$ dissociates, leading to an increase in the amount of $\mathrm{CH_3COO}^-$ (acetate) formed.

steps.title: Summary of Marks
steps.content:
In an IB exam, you earn marks for the following points:
1. Stating that $\mathrm{OH}^-$ reacts with $\mathrm{H}^+$ (neutralization).
2. Explaining that the system shifts to the right to replace the lost $\mathrm{H}^+$.
3. Identifying that this results in the formation of more $\mathrm{CH_3COO}^-$.

steps.title: Control Variable 1: Mass
steps.content:
To ensure a fair test, we must keep the amount of magnesium constant. The student uses **0.50 g of magnesium ribbon** in each trial. 

In terms of **Collision Theory** (Syllabus R2.2.3), controlling the mass (and the surface area/state of the ribbon) ensures that the total number of magnesium atoms available for collisions remains consistent across all concentrations. This prevents the total amount of gas produced from being affected by how much metal we started with.

steps.title: Control Variable 2: Time
steps.content:
The reaction rate is defined as the change in volume over time. In this experiment, the student measures the **Volume of H2(g) collected after 60 s**. 

By keeping the **reaction time** constant at $60\, \mathrm{s}$ for every trial, we can directly compare the volumes to determine which concentration produced gas the fastest. If the timing varied, we wouldn't know if a larger volume was due to a faster reaction or simply because we waited longer to measure it.

highlights.text: Volume of H2(g) collected after 60 s

steps.title: Identifying Kinetic Factors
steps.content:
While the markscheme highlights mass and time, the **Identification of Kinetic Factors** approach reminds us that **temperature** is also a critical variable to control. 

According to the **Arrhenius equation** and collision theory, higher temperatures increase the kinetic energy and collision frequency of particles. If the temperature were higher in the $2.00\, \mathrm{mol\,dm}^{-3}$ trial, the rate would increase not just because of the concentration, but also because the particles are moving faster. Keeping temperature constant ensures that the observed change in rate is solely due to the concentration of $\mathrm{HCl}$.

steps.title: Define controlled variables
steps.content:
To investigate the effect of concentration on the rate of reaction, we must ensure a "fair test." This means we only change one variable (the independent variable) while keeping all others constant. In this experiment, the **concentration of HCl** is our independent variable, so everything else related to the reactants and the measurement process must be controlled. 

This aligns with concepts found in **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**, where understanding factors affecting reaction rates is key.

steps.title: Control of magnesium mass
steps.content:
The first variable to control is the **mass of magnesium ribbon**. 

**Importance:** The question specifies that the student uses $0.50\, \mathrm{g}$ of magnesium. Keeping this mass constant ensures that the total amount of reactant is consistent across all trials. If the mass changed, it would alter the total possible volume of gas produced and the available surface area, making it impossible to determine if the change in rate was caused by the acid concentration or the amount of magnesium.

steps.title: Control of measurement time
steps.content:
The second variable to control is the **reaction time** (or the time at which the measurement is taken).

**Importance:** The student records the volume of $\mathrm{H}_2(g)$ specifically at $60\, \mathrm{s}$. Since the rate of reaction is calculated as the change in volume divided by time ($Rate = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta t}$), keeping the time interval identical across all trials allows for a direct and fair comparison of the gas volumes. If the time varied, a higher volume of gas wouldn't necessarily mean a faster reaction; it might just mean the reaction was allowed to run for longer.

highlights.text: Volume of H2(g) collected after 60 s

parts.content: Complete the table by calculating the average rate of reaction for each concentration of $\mathrm{HCl}$ and describe the relationship between the concentration of $\mathrm{HCl}$ and the average rate of reaction.
parts.markscheme:
Calculations for average rate:
- For $\mathrm{HCl}=0.50 \, \mathrm{mol} \, \mathrm{dm}^{-3}$:
$$
\text { Rate }=\frac{20.0}{60}=0.333 \, \mathrm{cm}^3 \, \mathrm{s}^{-1}
$$
- For $\mathrm{HCl}=1.00 \, \mathrm{mol} \, \mathrm{dm}^{-3}$:
$$
\text { Rate }=\frac{42.0}{60}=0.700 \, \mathrm{cm}^3 \, \mathrm{s}^{-1}
$$
- For $\mathrm{HCl}=1.50 \, \mathrm{mol} \, \mathrm{dm}^{-3}$:
$$
\text { Rate }=\frac{60.0}{60}=1.00 \, \mathrm{cm}^3 \, \mathrm{s}^{-1}
$$
- For $\mathrm{HCl}=2.00 \, \mathrm{mol} \, \mathrm{dm}^{-3}$:
$$
\text { Rate }=\frac{78.0}{60}=1.30 \, \mathrm{cm}^3 \, \mathrm{s}^{-1}
$$
<marks>2</marks>

Relationship:
- The rate of reaction increases as the concentration of $\mathrm{HCl}$ increases. <marks>1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Define the rate formula
steps.content:
To complete the table, we first need to understand how to calculate the average rate of reaction. Since the student measured the volume of hydrogen gas produced over a specific time interval, the rate is defined as the change in volume divided by the time elapsed.

$$\text{Rate of Reaction} = \frac{\text{Volume of } \mathrm{H}_2 \text{ collected}}{\text{Time interval}}$$

highlights.text: Volume of H2 gas collected after 60 s is recorded

steps.title: Calculate average rates
steps.content:
Using the constant time interval of $60\, \mathrm{s}$, we perform the arithmetic for each concentration trial. For IB Chemistry, it is important to maintain consistent significant figures (usually three, based on the provided data like $20.0$ or $0.50$):

$$\begin{aligned} \text{For } 0.50\, \mathrm{mol\,dm}^{-3} &= \frac{20.0\, \mathrm{cm}^3}{60\, \mathrm{s}} = 0.333\, \mathrm{cm}^3\, \mathrm{s}^{-1} \\ \text{For } 1.00\, \mathrm{mol\,dm}^{-3} &= \frac{42.0\, \mathrm{cm}^3}{60\, \mathrm{s}} = 0.700\, \mathrm{cm}^3\, \mathrm{s}^{-1} \\ \text{For } 1.50\, \mathrm{mol\,dm}^{-3} &= \frac{60.0\, \mathrm{cm}^3}{60\, \mathrm{s}} = 1.00\, \mathrm{cm}^3\, \mathrm{s}^{-1} \\ \text{For } 2.00\, \mathrm{mol\,dm}^{-3} &= \frac{78.0\, \mathrm{cm}^3}{60\, \mathrm{s}} = 1.30\, \mathrm{cm}^3\, \mathrm{s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Use the division key to compute volume/time for each row.

ti84.instructions: Enter 20/60, 42/60, 60/60, and 78/60 into the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the expressions for each trial to find the rate values.

calculator.description: Calculate the rates by dividing each volume by 60.

steps.title: Analyze the trend
steps.content:
Now, we observe the relationship between the independent variable (concentration) and the dependent variable (rate). 

Looking at our results:
- As the concentration of $\mathrm{HCl}$ increases from $0.50$ to $2.00\, \mathrm{mol\,dm}^{-3}$...
- The rate of reaction also increases from $0.333$ to $1.30\, \mathrm{cm}^3\, \mathrm{s}^{-1}$.

This shows a **positive correlation**. Therefore, the relationship is: as the concentration of $\mathrm{HCl}$ increases, the average rate of reaction increases.

steps.title: Review Markscheme requirements
steps.content:
In an IB exam, you earn marks for both the numerical accuracy and the clear description of the trend:
- **2 marks** are awarded for providing the four correct rate values (0.333, 0.700, 1.00, 1.30).
- **1 mark** is awarded for stating that the rate increases as the concentration increases.

Always ensure your final answer explicitly states this relationship to secure the final mark!

parts.content: Referring to the collision theory, explain the relationship outlined in Part 2.
parts.markscheme:
- Higher concentrations provide more frequent collisions between reactant particles per unit time, increasing the reaction rate. <marks>1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Define concentration as particle density
steps.content:
In chemistry, concentration refers to the amount of substance in a given volume. When we increase the concentration of hydrochloric acid ($\mathrm{HCl}$), we are increasing the number of reactive $\mathrm{H}^+$ ions present in the same volume of solution.

This concept is a fundamental part of **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**.

steps.title: Link particles to collision frequency
steps.content:
According to **collision theory**, for a reaction to occur, particles must collide with sufficient energy and correct orientation. 

Because there are now more $\mathrm{H}^+$ ions packed into the same space, the particles are closer together. This leads to a **higher frequency of collisions** (meaning there are more collisions occurring every second) between the magnesium ribbon and the hydrogen ions.

steps.title: Relate frequency to reaction rate
steps.content:
Since the reactant particles are colliding more often, the likelihood of successful collisions occurring per unit of time increases. This results in an overall increase in the rate of reaction, which explains why more $\mathrm{H}_2$ gas is collected in $60$ seconds at higher concentrations.

To earn the mark, you must mention that the **frequency of collisions** increases.

highlights.text: Higher concentrations provide more frequent collisions between reactant particles per unit time, increasing the reaction rate.

parts.content: The student finds that the collected gas volume is consistently lower than expected. Suggest one possible source of systematic error in the experiment and describe how it could be minimized.
parts.markscheme:
- **Source of error**: Some hydrogen gas may escape before the syringe starts collecting it (e.g., between adding acid and sealing the flask), leading to lower recorded volumes. <marks>1</marks>
- **Minimization**: Use a sealed reaction setup (e.g., a divided flask or adding acid via a dropping funnel through a bung) to ensure that no gas escapes before it is measured. <marks>1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the gas loss
steps.content:
A common systematic error in gas collection experiments is the loss of gas at the very start of the reaction. 

When you add the magnesium ribbon to the $\mathrm{HCl}$, the reaction starts **instantly**. There is always a small time delay between dropping the magnesium into the flask and pushing the rubber bung into the neck to seal it. During those few seconds, some $\mathrm{H}_2$ gas escapes into the atmosphere instead of being directed into the gas syringe. This results in a consistently lower volume reading than the true value.

highlights.text: collected gas volume is consistently lower than expected
Some hydrogen gas may escape before the syringe starts collecting it (e.g., between adding acid and sealing the flask)

steps.title: Use a sealed setup
steps.content:
To minimize this error, we must ensure the system is completely sealed *before* the reactants come into contact. Two effective ways to do this are:

1.  **Using a divided flask (two-arm flask):** Place the magnesium in one side and the acid in the other. Seal the flask with the bung and syringe, then tilt the flask to mix the reactants.
2.  **Using a dropping funnel:** Place the magnesium in a flask and seal it with a bung that has a dropping funnel attached. You can then add the $\mathrm{HCl}$ through the funnel, which keeps the system closed throughout the process.

By using these methods, you capture the $\mathrm{H}_2$ gas from the very first moment the reaction begins.

highlights.text: Use a sealed reaction setup (e.g., a divided flask or adding acid via a dropping funnel through a bung)

steps.title: Identify the systematic error
steps.content:
The question notes that the gas volume is consistently lower than the theoretical expectations. One chemical reason for this is that magnesium is a reactive metal that often has a layer of magnesium oxide ($\text{MgO}$) on its surface due to reaction with oxygen in the air.

Since the student measured exactly $0.50\, \text{g}$ of the ribbon, a portion of that mass is actually magnesium oxide rather than pure magnesium metal.

highlights.text: collected gas volume is consistently lower than expected

steps.title: Explain the missing gas
steps.content:
The reason this leads to a lower volume is based on the chemical reaction. While magnesium reacts with $\text{HCl}$ to produce hydrogen gas:

$$\text{Mg}(s) + 2\text{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2(aq) + \text{H}_2(g)$$

The magnesium oxide layer reacts with $\text{HCl}$ but does **not** produce hydrogen gas. Instead, it produces water:

$$\text{MgO}(s) + 2\text{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$$

Because some of the mass used was $\text{MgO}$, there was less actual $\text{Mg}$ available to produce $\text{H}_2$ gas.

steps.title: Minimize the error
steps.content:
To minimize this systematic error, the student should clean the magnesium ribbon before weighing it. 

Using **sandpaper** or emery paper to rub the surface of the ribbon removes the dull oxide layer, ensuring that the $0.50\, \text{g}$ used in the experiment is as close to $100\%$ pure magnesium as possible.

steps.title: Final Summary
steps.content:
To get full marks for this specific approach:
1. **Source of error**: The presence of an oxide layer ($\text{MgO}$) on the magnesium ribbon which does not produce $\text{H}_2$ gas. (1 mark)
2. **Minimization**: Clean the magnesium ribbon with sandpaper/emery paper before use. (1 mark)

steps.title: Identify the systematic error
steps.content:
The question states that the volume of $H_2$ gas is **consistently lower** than expected. This indicates a **systematic error**—a flaw in the equipment or procedure that shifts all measurements in the same direction. 

One common source of error when using a gas syringe is the internal friction between the syringe plunger and the barrel.

highlights.text: collected gas volume is consistently lower than expected
one possible source of systematic error

steps.title: Mechanism of volume reduction
steps.content:
In an ideal experiment, the gas produced should push the plunger out freely until the internal pressure equals the external pressure. However, if there is significant friction:

1. The gas must exert extra pressure to overcome the resistance of the plunger.
2. The plunger may "stick" and stop moving before it reaches the true volume of gas produced.

This results in a recorded volume that is lower than the actual volume of gas generated by the reaction between $Mg$ and $HCl$.

steps.title: Minimizing the error
steps.content:
To minimize the effect of friction and ensure the plunger moves more freely, we can:

*   **Lubricate the syringe**: Applying a very small amount of low-viscosity lubricant (like thin oil or graphite) to the plunger can reduce resistance.
*   **Use different apparatus**: Switch to a high-quality, ground-glass gas syringe, which typically has much lower friction than plastic versions.
*   **Clean the equipment**: Ensure the syringe is perfectly clean and dry, as grit or moisture can increase friction.

steps.title: Final Answer Summary
steps.content:
Based on the marks awarded in IB experiments for this topic:

*   **Source of error**: Internal friction between the gas syringe plunger and the barrel, which prevents the plunger from reaching the correct volume.
*   **Minimization**: Lubricate the syringe plunger or use a low-friction glass gas syringe.

$$\mathrm{N_2O_4(g)} \rightleftharpoons 2\mathrm{NO_2(g)} \quad \Delta H = +57\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

At a temperature of $350\text{ K}$, the equilibrium constant $K_c$ for this reaction is $0.10$.

A reaction vessel at $350\text{ K}$ contains the concentrations shown in the table below:

| Species | Concentration / $\text{mol dm}^{-3}$ |

Determine whether the system is currently at equilibrium. Justify your answer using a calculation of the reaction quotient, $Q_c$.

- Calculate the value of $Q_c$: $Q_c = \dfrac{[\mathrm{NO_2}]^2}{[\mathrm{N_2O_4}]} = \dfrac{(0.20)^2}{0.50} = 0.08$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
A student investigates the production of gas during the reaction of magnesium with hydrochloric acid:

$$
\text{Mg(s)} + 2\text{HCl(aq)} \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2\text{(aq)} + \text{H}_2\text{(g)}
$$

The pressure of the gas ($P$) is measured at regular time intervals ($t$). The following data were recorded:

| Time ($t$) (s) | Pressure ($P$) (kPa) |
| :--- | :--- |
| 0 | 100 |
| 10 | 110 |
| 20 | 120 |
| 30 | 130 |
| 40 | 140 |
| 50 | 145 |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.2 How fast? The rate of chemical change > R2.2.1 Rate of reaction
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.1 Rate of reaction
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Main task is plotting pressure–time data and using the slope/gradient to determine reaction rate at a specific time, including interpreting the significance of slope.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.2 How fast? The rate of chemical change > R2.2.10 Order of a reaction (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.10 Order of a reaction (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (f) explicitly states to assume first-order kinetics, connecting the rate behavior/graph interpretation to reaction order.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.2 How fast? The rate of chemical change > R2.2.3 Factors affecting the rate of reaction
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.3 Factors affecting the rate of reaction
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) asks why pressure may not increase linearly with time, which is typically explained by changing rate due to factors like reactant depletion, surface area changes, temperature, etc.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 3. What are the mechanisms of chemical change? > R3.2 Electron transfer reactions > R3.2.4 Acid-metal reactions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R3.2.4 Acid-metal reactions

parts.content: Explain why the pressure might not increase linearly with time in this experiment.
parts.markscheme:
- State that the concentration of $\text{HCl}$ decreases as the reaction progresses, leading to a decrease in the rate of reaction/collision frequency. <marks>A1</marks>
- State that the surface area of the magnesium decreases as it is consumed, also reducing the reaction rate. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the data trend
steps.content:
A linear increase in pressure would mean that the rate of gas production is constant over time. If we look at the data table, the pressure increases by $10\text{ kPa}$ every $10$ seconds initially (from $0$ to $40\text{ s}$). However, between $40\text{ s}$ and $50\text{ s}$, the pressure only increases by $5\text{ kPa}$.

This change indicates that the **rate of reaction** is slowing down. To understand why, we look at **Topic R2.2: Collision theory**.

steps.title: Decrease in reactant concentration
steps.content:
As the reaction progresses, the $\text{HCl(aq)}$ is consumed to produce $\text{MgCl}_2\text{(aq)}$ and $\text{H}_2\text{(g)}$. 

According to collision theory, a reaction occurs when particles collide with sufficient energy and correct orientation. As the concentration of $\text{HCl}$ decreases, there are fewer reactant particles per unit volume. This leads to a **lower frequency of successful collisions** between the $\text{H}^+$ ions and the magnesium metal, which reduces the reaction rate.

highlights.text: concentration of HCl decreases as the reaction progresses, leading to a decrease in the rate of reaction/collision frequency

steps.title: Decrease in surface area
steps.content:
The magnesium ($\text{Mg}$) is a solid reactant. As the reaction continues, the magnesium is used up, causing the size of the magnesium pieces to shrink. 

This results in a **decrease in the surface area** available for the $\text{HCl}$ particles to collide with. Since there is less surface for collisions to occur on, the frequency of collisions decreases further, contributing to the slowing down of the reaction over time.

highlights.text: surface area of the magnesium decreases as it is consumed, also reducing the reaction rate

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content:
Because the concentration of $\text{HCl}$ and the surface area of $\text{Mg}$ both decrease as the reaction proceeds, the overall rate of reaction slows down. 

Therefore, the rate of $\text{H}_2\text{(g)}$ production decreases over time, and the graph of pressure versus time becomes a curve rather than a straight line (non-linear).

steps.title: Link rate to pressure
steps.content: In this experiment, the rate of reaction is proportional to the rate at which the pressure increases. If the reaction rate were constant, the pressure would increase linearly (by the same amount every 10 seconds). However, the data shows that between 40 and 50 seconds, the pressure only increases by $5 \text{ kPa}$, whereas it increased by $10 \text{ kPa}$ in earlier intervals.

steps.title: Analyze the solid reactant
steps.content:
Using a **Surface Area Analysis**, we look at the physical state of the magnesium. The reaction equation shows that magnesium is a solid:

$$\text{Mg(s)} + 2\text{HCl(aq)} \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2\text{(aq)} + \text{H}_2\text{(g)}$$

As the reaction proceeds, the magnesium ribbon is consumed and converted into aqueous magnesium chloride. This means the actual size of the magnesium pieces decreases over time.

steps.title: Connect area to collisions
steps.content:
According to **Collision Theory** (which you can review in **Topic R2.2**), the reaction can only occur at the surface of the solid where the $\text{HCl}$ particles can collide with $\text{Mg}$ atoms. 

As the magnesium is used up, its total **surface area decreases**. With less surface area available, there are fewer "active sites" for collisions to occur, leading to a lower frequency of successful collisions and a slower reaction rate.

steps.title: Consider the acid concentration
steps.content:
To ensure we satisfy the markscheme for a full 2 marks, we must also mention the other reactant. As $\text{HCl}$ reacts, its **concentration decreases**. 

Lower concentration means there are fewer $\text{H}^+$ ions per unit volume, which also contributes to a lower collision frequency and a non-linear decrease in the rate of pressure change.

parts.content: Suggest two experimental improvements to reduce errors and ensure accurate pressure measurements.
parts.markscheme:
- Suggest using a water bath to maintain a constant temperature, as pressure is temperature-dependent. <marks>A1</marks>
- Suggest ensuring the apparatus is completely gas-tight/sealed to prevent gas loss. <marks>A1</marks>
- Suggest using a gas syringe for volume measurement as a check, or use a higher precision pressure sensor. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Accept any two valid improvements.</note>

steps.title: Analyze the Ideal Gas Law
steps.content:
To understand how to improve this experiment, we look at the **Ideal Gas Law** equation from your data booklet. In this reaction, we are measuring the pressure ($P$) of the hydrogen gas produced to determine the rate of reaction. 

$$\text{PV} = \text{nRT}$$

If we want the change in pressure ($P$) to accurately reflect only the change in the amount of gas ($n$), we must ensure that the volume ($V$) and the temperature ($T$) remain constant throughout the experiment.

steps.title: Control the temperature (T)
steps.content:
The reaction between magnesium and hydrochloric acid is **exothermic**, meaning it releases heat. According to the relationship $$P \propto T$$ (at constant volume and moles), an increase in temperature will cause the pressure to rise independently of the gas being produced.

**Improvement:** Use a **thermostatic water bath** to keep the reaction mixture at a constant temperature. This ensures that any pressure increase is due to the production of gas ($n$) rather than a change in temperature ($T$).

highlights.text: Suggest using a water bath to maintain a constant temperature

steps.title: Ensure a gas-tight system
steps.content:
In the equation $$PV = nRT$$, if gas escapes the apparatus, the number of moles ($n$) in the container decreases. This leads to a lower pressure reading than what was actually produced by the reaction.

**Improvement:** Ensure the entire apparatus is **completely gas-tight and sealed** (e.g., using vacuum grease on joints). This prevents gas loss and ensures that the pressure sensor measures all the hydrogen gas generated.

highlights.text: Suggest ensuring the apparatus is completely gas-tight/sealed

steps.title: Final answer summary
steps.content:
Based on our analysis of the variables in the Ideal Gas Law, the two best improvements are:

1. Use a **water bath** to maintain a constant temperature ($T$).
2. Ensure the apparatus is **gas-tight** to prevent the loss of gas ($n$).

These adjustments isolate the relationship between pressure and the amount of gas, leading to more accurate data.

steps.title: Evaluate system integrity
steps.content:
To ensure accurate pressure measurements, we must first evaluate the physical integrity of the experimental setup. In a gas-production reaction, the most common systematic error is a **gas leak**. If the apparatus is not perfectly sealed, some $H_2$ gas will escape, causing the recorded pressure to be lower than the theoretical value.

An improvement is to ensure the apparatus is completely **gas-tight**. This can be achieved by using high-quality rubber bungs or applying vacuum grease to the joints of the glassware to prevent any gas loss.

highlights.text: The pressure of the gas ($P$) is measured

steps.title: Control temperature variables
steps.content:
According to the Ideal Gas Law, pressure is directly proportional to temperature when volume and the number of moles are constant:

$$PV = nRT$$

Because the reaction between magnesium and hydrochloric acid is exothermic, it releases heat into the surroundings. This temperature increase causes the gas particles to move faster and hit the walls of the container with more force, artificially inflating the pressure reading. To isolate the pressure change due solely to gas production, you should use a **thermostatic water bath** to maintain a constant temperature throughout the experiment.

steps.title: Summary of improvements
steps.content:
Based on the lab procedure evaluation, the two best improvements to ensure accuracy are:

1.  **Ensure a gas-tight seal:** Use vacuum grease on joints to prevent systematic errors from gas leaks.
2.  **Use a water bath:** Maintain a constant temperature to ensure the pressure change is only due to the moles of $H_2$ produced, as per the relationship in **Topic S1.5 Ideal Gases**.

steps.title: Analyze variables and relationships
steps.content:
To optimize the measurements, we first need to understand how the pressure reading ($P$) is influenced by other factors in the experiment. According to the **Ideal Gas Law** found in your data booklet, the variables are related as follows:

$$PV = nRT$$

In this reaction, as the moles of gas ($n$) increase, the pressure ($P$) increases. However, if the temperature ($T$) or volume ($V$) fluctuates unexpectedly, the pressure readings from our sensor will include errors. This question is covered in **Topic S1.5: Ideal Gases**, which explores these relationships.

steps.title: Upgrade sensor resolution
steps.content:
The data provided shows pressure values in large increments (mostly 10 kPa). To reduce **random error** and improve the sensitivity of the measurements, you should use a **higher precision pressure sensor**.

For example, a sensor with a resolution of $\pm 0.1\text{ kPa}$ instead of $\pm 1\text{ kPa}$ would provide much more detailed data points, especially between 40s and 50s where the rate appears to slow down.

highlights.text: Suggest using a gas syringe for volume measurement as a check, or use a higher precision pressure sensor.

steps.title: Add alternative verification tools
steps.content:
A great way to ensure the accuracy of your primary instrument (the pressure sensor) is to use an **alternative verification tool**. 

Suggest using a **gas syringe** to measure the volume of hydrogen gas produced. By comparing the volume of gas in the syringe to the pressure increase in the flask, you can verify that the pressure change is solely due to the chemical reaction and not a leak or a sensor calibration error.

highlights.text: Suggest using a gas syringe for volume measurement as a check, or use a higher precision pressure sensor.

steps.title: Maintain environmental stability
steps.content:
Instrumentation sensitivity means that even small temperature changes can affect the pressure output. To ensure the pressure readings are accurate, you should use a **water bath** to maintain a constant temperature throughout the reaction.

Since $P \propto T$ (Gay-Lussac's Law), keeping the temperature stable prevents thermal expansion of the gas from being recorded as a false increase in pressure from the reaction.

highlights.text: Suggest using a water bath to maintain a constant temperature, as pressure is temperature-dependent.

parts.content: Plot the graph of $P$ vs $t$ using the provided data.
parts.markscheme:
- Label both axes correctly with units ($t$ in $\text{s}$ on x-axis, $P$ in $\text{kPa}$ on y-axis). <marks>A1</marks>
- Plot all points correctly and draw a smooth curve or line of best fit that shows a decreasing gradient at higher $t$. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

![Graph](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/830a2f96-068c-4577-9c34-b32ea161bfc1.png)

steps.title: Identify variables and axes
steps.content:
To plot a scientific graph, we first identify the independent and dependent variables. 

*   **Independent variable (x-axis):** Time ($t$) in seconds (s).
*   **Dependent variable (y-axis):** Pressure ($P$) in kilopascals (kPa).

Make sure to include the units in your labels, as this is a requirement for the first mark.

highlights.text: Label both axes correctly with units

steps.title: Choose an appropriate scale
steps.content:
Next, we select a scale that allows the data to fill at least half of the graph paper.

*   **x-axis:** The time ranges from $0$ to $50$ s. A scale of 1 large square = 10 s works well.
*   **y-axis:** The pressure starts at $100$ kPa and reaches $145$ kPa. You can start your y-axis at $100$ or use a zigzag (break) if necessary, but starting at $0$ or $90$ is often clearer. Let's use a scale that comfortably reaches $150$ kPa.

steps.title: Plot the coordinate pairs
steps.content:
Using the data table, plot each point accurately as $(t, P)$. 

$$\begin{aligned} &(0, 100), \\ &(10, 110), \\ &(20, 120), \\ &(30, 130), \\ &(40, 140), \\ &(50, 145) \end{aligned}$$

Notice that the first 5 points show a steady increase of $10$ kPa every $10$ s, while the last point only increases by $5$ kPa. This indicates the reaction rate is slowing down.

steps.title: Draw the best-fit line
steps.content:
Finally, draw a smooth line through the points. 

1.  For the first $40$ seconds, the relationship is linear (a straight line).
2.  At $t = 50$, the gradient decreases. You must draw a **smooth curve** that starts to flatten out between $40$ s and $50$ s to show this decreasing rate.

This reflects the chemistry: as reactants are used up, the rate of gas production (and thus pressure increase) slows down.

parts.content: Suggest how the graph can be used to determine the reaction rate at $t=20 \text{ s}$ and explain the significance of the slope.
parts.markscheme:
- Describe drawing a tangent to the curve at $t = 20 \text{ s}$ and calculating the gradient/slope $(\Delta P / \Delta t)$. <marks>A1</marks>
- State that the slope represents the instantaneous rate of reaction in units of $\text{kPa s}^{-1}$. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the Rate Type
steps.content: To find the reaction rate at a specific moment in time ($t = 20 \text{ s}$), we are looking for the **instantaneous rate**. Since the pressure-time graph is a curve, we cannot simply use the average rate; instead, we must find the steepness of the curve at that exact point. This concept is explored in **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**.

steps.title: Draw the Tangent
steps.content: First, plot the data points and draw a smooth curve of best fit. To determine the rate at exactly $t = 20 \text{ s}$, place a ruler against the curve at that specific point. Draw a straight line (the **tangent**) that just touches the curve at $t = 20 \text{ s}$ and has the same steepness as the curve at that point.

steps.title: Calculate the Gradient
steps.content:
Next, calculate the **gradient** (slope) of this tangent line. Pick two points on the tangent line $(t_1, P_1)$ and $(t_2, P_2)$ and use the formula:

$$\text{Gradient} = \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta t} = \frac{P_2 - P_1}{t_2 - t_1}$$

This represents the change in pressure divided by the change in time at that specific moment.

gdc.instructions: In the calculation menu, perform (130 - 110) / (30 - 10).

ti84.instructions: Enter (130 - 110) / (30 - 10) and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type (130 - 110) / (30 - 10) into an expression cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the gradient using points around t=20 from the data table (since the curve appears linear there).

steps.title: Explain the Significance
steps.content: The value of this slope is the **instantaneous rate of reaction**. It tells us how quickly gas is being produced at that exact second. Based on the axes of our graph, the units for this rate will be **$\text{kPa s}^{-1}$**.

highlights.text: instantaneous rate of reaction in units of \text{kPa s}^{-1}

parts.content: Using the graph, determine the reaction rate ($r$) at $t = 20 \text{ s}$ in terms of $\text{kPa s}^{-1}$. Assume the reaction follows first-order kinetics.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify two points on the tangent at $t = 20 \text{ s}$, e.g., $(15, 115)$ and $(25, 125)$. <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the gradient: $r = \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta t} = \frac{125 - 115}{25 - 15} = \frac{10}{10}$. <marks>A1</marks>
- State final value with units: $r = 1.0 \text{ kPa s}^{-1}$. <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify the point
steps.content:
To find the instantaneous rate of reaction at a specific time, we look at the graph of Pressure ($P$) versus Time ($t$). 

From the data table, we can see that at $t = 20\text{ s}$, the pressure is $120\text{ kPa}$. This gives us the point $(20, 120)$ on our curve. This concept is covered in **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**.

steps.title: Draw the tangent line
steps.content:
Using the **Tangent Gradient Method**, we draw a straight line that just touches the curve at exactly $t = 20\text{ s}$. 

The gradient (slope) of this tangent line represents the instantaneous rate of change of pressure, which is proportional to the reaction rate ($r$).

steps.title: Select coordinates
steps.content:
To calculate the gradient, we need two points $(t, P)$ that lie on the tangent line we just drew. 

Looking at the tangent line, we can identify two clear points. For example, let's use:
- Point 1: $(15, 115)$
- Point 2: $(25, 125)$

steps.title: Calculate the gradient
steps.content:
The reaction rate ($r$) is the gradient of the tangent line. We use the gradient formula:

$$r = \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta t} = \frac{P_2 - P_1}{t_2 - t_1}$$

Substituting our values:

$$r = \frac{125 - 115}{25 - 15} = \frac{10}{10} = 1.0$$

gdc.instructions: In the main calculation menu, perform (125-115)/(25-15).

ti84.instructions: Enter (125-115)/(25-15) on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into a cell to find the result.

calculator.description: Calculate the gradient using the chosen coordinates.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The reaction rate at $t = 20\text{ s}$ is **$1.0\text{ kPa s}^{-1}$**.

Make sure to include the units, which are derived from the axes: pressure (kPa) divided by time (s).

steps.title: Identify the target time
steps.content:
To find the rate of reaction at a specific point in time using the average rate approach, we look for data points in the table that surround our target time. This concept is covered in **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**.

Our goal is to determine the rate at $t = 20 \text{ s}$.

steps.title: Select a symmetrical interval
steps.content:
To get the most accurate estimate of the instantaneous rate at $t = 20 \text{ s}$ using the table, we choose a small, symmetrical interval around that time. 

Looking at the provided data table, the closest symmetrical points around $20 \text{ s}$ are at $t = 10 \text{ s}$ and $t = 30 \text{ s}$:

*   At $t_1 = 10 \text{ s}$, $P_1 = 110 \text{ kPa}$
*   At $t_2 = 30 \text{ s}$, $P_2 = 130 \text{ kPa}$

steps.title: Calculate the average rate
steps.content:
The reaction rate $r$ is the change in pressure ($\Delta P$) divided by the change in time ($\Delta t$). We apply the gradient formula:

$$r = \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta t} = \frac{P_2 - P_1}{t_2 - t_1}$$

Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} r &= \frac{130 - 110}{30 - 10} \\ r &= \frac{20}{20} \\ r &= 1.0 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Use the division operation to calculate (130 - 110) divided by (30 - 10).

ti84.instructions: Enter (130 - 110) / (30 - 10) on the home screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into a cell to find the result.

calculator.description: Calculate the gradient using the points (10, 110) and (30, 130).

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculation gives us a rate of $1.0$. We must include the units specified in the question.

$$r = 1.0 \text{ kPa s}^{-1}$$

parts.content: If the uncertainty in pressure is $\pm 1.0 \text{ kPa}$ and the uncertainty in time is $\pm 0.5 \text{ s}$, calculate the percentage uncertainty in the reaction rate determined at $t = 20 \text{ s}$ using the points $(15, 115)$ and $(25, 125)$.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the percentage uncertainty in $\Delta P$: $\frac{1.0 + 1.0}{125 - 115} \times 100 = 20\%$. <marks>A1</marks>
- Calculate the percentage uncertainty in $\Delta t$: $\frac{0.5 + 0.5}{25 - 15} \times 100 = 10\%$. <marks>A1</marks>
- Determine the total percentage uncertainty in the rate ($r$): $20\% + 10\% = 30\%$. <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Define the rate formula
steps.content:
To find the reaction rate, we calculate the change in pressure over the change in time. The formula for the rate ($r$) is:

$$r = \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta t} = \frac{P_2 - P_1}{t_2 - t_1}$$

When calculating uncertainties for a quotient (division), IB rules require us to **add the percentage (relative) uncertainties** of the components involved.

steps.title: Uncertainty in Pressure change
steps.content:
First, we find the uncertainty in the pressure difference ($\Delta P$). When we subtract two values, we add their absolute uncertainties.

$$\Delta P = 125 - 115 = 10 \text{ kPa}$$
$$\text{Absolute uncertainty} = 1.0 + 1.0 = 2.0 \text{ kPa}$$

Now, convert this to a percentage uncertainty:

$$\% \text{ uncertainty in } \Delta P = \frac{2.0}{10} \times 100 = 20\%$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the ratio of the total absolute uncertainty to the pressure difference and multiply by 100.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the expression for percentage uncertainty.

calculator.description: Calculate the percentage uncertainty for the pressure difference.

steps.title: Uncertainty in Time change
steps.content:
Next, we find the uncertainty in the time difference ($\Delta t$). Just like with pressure, we add the absolute uncertainties of the individual measurements.

$$\Delta t = 25 - 15 = 10 \text{ s}$$
$$\text{Absolute uncertainty} = 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.0 \text{ s}$$

Now, convert this to a percentage uncertainty:

$$\% \text{ uncertainty in } \Delta t = \frac{1.0}{10} \times 100 = 10\%$$

gdc.instructions: Divide the summed absolute uncertainty by the time difference and multiply by 100.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the expression for percentage uncertainty in time.

calculator.description: Calculate the percentage uncertainty for the time difference.

steps.title: Calculate total uncertainty
steps.content:
The reaction rate is calculated using division ($r = \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta t}$), so we add the percentage uncertainties of the numerator and the denominator together to find the total relative uncertainty.

$$\text{Total } \% \text{ uncertainty} = 20\% + 10\% = 30\%$$

This final value represents the combined experimental error for the determined rate.

gdc.instructions: Add the previously calculated percentage values.

ti84.instructions: Enter 20 + 10

desmos.instructions: Add the percentage results.

calculator.description: Sum the two percentage uncertainties.

parts.content: Draw the Lewis structure for the nitrite ion ($NO_2^-$), ensuring that all valence electrons and the overall charge are clearly indicated.

parts.content: Calculate the formal charge of each atom in one of the resonance structures of the $NO_2^-$ ion. Show your working.

- Nitrogen: $5 - 2 - \frac{1}{2}(6) = 0$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Double-bonded Oxygen: $6 - 4 - \frac{1}{2}(4) = 0$ AND Single-bonded Oxygen: $6 - 6 - \frac{1}{2}(2) = -1$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
A student uses a calorimetry setup to determine the enthalpy of combustion of propan-1-ol ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$). The experimental data collected is shown in the table below:

| Initial mass of spirit burner + propan-1-ol | $124.65 \text{ g}$ |
| Final mass of spirit burner + propan-1-ol | $123.92 \text{ g}$ |

Specific heat capacity of water, $c = 4.18 \text{ J g}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$.

- $\Delta H_c = -\frac{15.36}{0.01214} = -1265 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ (accept $-1264$ to $-1266$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
Calculate the enthalpy change for the hydration of ethene to ethanol using the standard enthalpies of formation provided:

$\text{C}_2\text{H}_4(g) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(g) \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}(l)$

- $\Delta H_f [\text{C}_2\text{H}_4(g)] = +52 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$
- $\Delta H_f [\text{H}_2\text{O}(g)] = -242 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$
- $\Delta H_f [\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}(l)] = -278 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$
parts.markscheme:
- $\Delta H = \sum \Delta H_f(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H_f(\text{reactants})$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $\Delta H = (-278) - [52 + (-242)] = -88 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
The enthalpy of combustion of methane can be estimated using average bond enthalpies. Given the data below, calculate the enthalpy change for: $\text{CH}_4(g) + 2\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{CO}_2(g) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(g)$

| Bond | Average Bond Enthalpy ($\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$) |
| :--- | :--- |
| C-H | 413 |
| O=O | 498 |
| C=O | 805 |
| O-H | 464 |
parts.markscheme:
- Bonds broken $= 4(413) + 2(498) = 2648 \text{ kJ}$; Bonds formed $= 2(805) + 4(464) = 3466 \text{ kJ}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $\Delta H = 2648 - 3466 = -818 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: Which of the following factors primarily determines the extent of a chemical change in a reaction at equilibrium?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.3 How far? The extent of chemical change > R2.3.2 The equilibrium law and constant
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.3.2 The equilibrium law and constant
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks what primarily determines the extent of chemical change at equilibrium, which is governed by the equilibrium position/extent described quantitatively by the equilibrium law and equilibrium constant K.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.3 How far? The extent of chemical change > R2.3.3 Magnitude of K and temperature dependence
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.3.3 Magnitude of K and temperature dependence
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Among the listed factors, temperature changes K (temperature dependence), directly affecting equilibrium extent; this links to how K varies with temperature.

options.markscheme: Temperature is the only factor that changes the value of the equilibrium constant ($K_c$) for a given reaction. The magnitude of $K_c$ indicates the extent of the reaction.

Pressure can shift the position of equilibrium for reactions involving gases with different numbers of moles on each side, but it does not change the equilibrium constant (the extent of the reaction).

Changing the concentration of reactants or products shifts the position of equilibrium according to Le Chatelier’s principle, but the value of the equilibrium constant remains the same at a constant temperature.

While the nature of the reactants determines the standard Gibbs free energy change and thus the initial value of $K_c$ for a specific reaction, temperature is the primary external factor that determines the extent of reaction for any given chemical equilibrium system.

parts.content:
Ammonia reacts reversibly with water.

$$\mathrm{NH_3(g) + H_2O(l) \rightleftharpoons NH_4^+(aq) + OH^-(aq)}$$

Explain the effect of adding $\mathrm{H^+(aq)}$ ions on the position of the equilibrium.
parts.markscheme:
- Removes/reacts with $\mathrm{OH^-}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Moves to the right/products «to replace $\mathrm{OH^-}$ ions» <marks>A1</marks>

<note>Accept ionic equation for M1.</note>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the chemical reaction
steps.content:
When we add $\mathrm{H^+(aq)}$ ions to the mixture, they will react with the $\mathrm{OH^-(aq)}$ ions already present in the equilibrium. This is a fundamental neutralization reaction which produces water:

$$\mathrm{H^+(aq) + OH^-(aq) \rightarrow H_2O(l)}$$

Because of this reaction, the concentration of $\mathrm{OH^-(aq)}$ ions in the solution decreases. This concept is covered in **Topic R3.1: Proton transfer reactions**.

steps.title: Apply Le Chatelier's Principle
steps.content:
According to **Le Chatelier's Principle** (Topic R2.3.4), if a system at equilibrium is disturbed by a change in concentration, the system will shift its equilibrium position to oppose that change.

Since the concentration of the product $\mathrm{OH^-(aq)}$ has been reduced, the system will try to 'replace' the lost ions. To do this, the equilibrium position shifts to the **right** (towards the products side).

This shift results in more $\mathrm{NH_3}$ reacting with $\mathrm{H_2O}$ to restore the balance.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
To summarize for your exam marks:
1. The added $\mathrm{H^+}$ reacts with and removes $\mathrm{OH^-}$ ions (M1).
2. The equilibrium shifts to the **right** to replace the removed $\mathrm{OH^-}$ ions (A1).

steps.title: Define the equilibrium constant
steps.content:
First, let's write the equilibrium constant expression ($K_c$) for the given reaction. In this reaction, ammonia gas reacts with water to form ammonium and hydroxide ions:

$$\mathrm{NH_3(g) + H_2O(l) \rightleftharpoons NH_4^+(aq) + OH^-(aq)}$$

The equilibrium constant expression is the ratio of the concentration of products to reactants at equilibrium:

$$K_c = \frac{[\mathrm{NH_4^+}][\mathrm{OH^-}]}{[\mathrm{NH_3}]}$$

Note: We exclude $\mathrm{H_2O(l)}$ because it is a pure liquid and its concentration remains essentially constant.

steps.title: Reacting H+ with OH-
steps.content:
When we add $\mathrm{H^+(aq)}$ ions to the system, they will immediately react with the $\mathrm{OH^-(aq)}$ ions already present in the solution. This is a neutralization reaction:

$$\mathrm{H^+(aq) + OH^-(aq) \rightarrow H_2O(l)}$$

This reaction effectively **removes $\mathrm{OH^-}$ ions** from the equilibrium mixture, causing their concentration to decrease.

steps.title: Analyze the reaction quotient
steps.content:
Because the concentration of $\mathrm{OH^-}$ has decreased, the system is no longer at equilibrium. We can describe the current state using the reaction quotient, $Q$:

$$Q = \frac{[\mathrm{NH_4^+}][\mathrm{OH^-}]_{\text{new}}}{[\mathrm{NH_3}]}$$

Since $[\mathrm{OH^-}]_{\text{new}}$ is smaller than the equilibrium concentration, the value of $Q$ becomes less than $K_c$ ($Q < K_c$).

steps.title: Determine the equilibrium shift
steps.content:
To restore equilibrium, the system must increase $Q$ until it equals $K_c$ again. This can only happen if the concentration of the products increases and the concentration of the reactants decreases.

Therefore, the position of the **equilibrium shifts to the right** (towards the products) to replace the lost $\mathrm{OH^-}$ ions.

questions.specification: A reversible reaction is at equilibrium. When the temperature is increased, the equilibrium constant $K_c$ decreases.
